100.section_title: Right of private defence against deadly assault when there is risk of harm to innocent person

raw.marginal_note: Right of private defence against deadly assault when there is risk of harm to innocent person
raw.text: If, in the exercise of the right of private defence against an assault which causes the defender to reasonably believe that death would be caused to him or to any other person and the defender is so situated that he cannot effectually exercise that right without risk of harm to an innocent person, his right of private defence extends to the running of that risk.

sub_items.text: Illustration

A is attacked by a mob who attempt to murder him. A cannot effectually exercise his right of private defence without firing on the mob, and he cannot fire without risk of harming young children who are mingled with the mob. A commits no offence if by so firing he harms any of the children.

elements_detail.label: exercise_of_right
elements_detail.text: In the exercise of the right of private defence

elements_detail.label: assault_causing_reasonable_belief_of_death
elements_detail.text: Against an assault which causes the defender to reasonably believe that death would be caused to him or to any other person

elements_detail.label: cannot_effectually_exercise_without_risk
elements_detail.text: The defender is so situated that he cannot effectually exercise that right without risk of harm to an innocent person

elements_detail.label: extension_to_running_risk
elements_detail.text: His right of private defence extends to the running of that risk

Illustrations:

A cannot effectually exercise his right of private defence without firing on the mob, and he cannot fire without risk of harming young children who are mingled with the mob. A commits no offence if by so firing he harms any of the children.

101.section_title: Start and continuance of right of private defence of body

raw.marginal_note: Start and continuance of right of private defence of body
raw.text: —(1) The right of private defence of the body starts as soon as the defender reasonably believes that there is danger to the body (either his own or that of any other person) arising from any act which is an offence against the human body or an attempt or a threat to commit the offence, though the offence may not have been committed. (2) The right of private defence continues as long as the belief of danger mentioned in subsection (1) continues.

sub_items.text: —(1) The right of private defence of the body starts as soon as the defender reasonably believes that there is danger to the body (either his own or that of any other person) arising from any act which is an offence against the human body or an attempt or a threat to commit the offence, though the offence may not have been committed.
(2) The right of private defence continues as long as the belief of danger mentioned in subsection (1) continues.

102.section_title: When right of private defence of body extends to causing death

raw.marginal_note: When right of private defence of body extends to causing death
raw.text: The right of private defence of the body extends, under the restrictions mentioned in sections 98 and 106A, to the voluntary causing of death to the assailant, if the offence which gives rise to the exercise of the right is of any of the following descriptions: ( a ) an assault where the defender reasonably believes that death will otherwise be the consequence of such assault; ( b ) an assault where the defender reasonably believes that grievous hurt will otherwise be the consequence of such assault; ( c ) an assault that the defender reasonably believes to be done with the intention of committing rape as described in section 375 or causing such rape to be committed; ( d ) an assault that the defender reasonably believes to be done with the intention of causing penile penetration of the vagina, anus or mouth as described in section 376(2); ( e ) an assault that the defender reasonably believes to be done with the intention of kidnapping or abducting; ( f ) an assault that the defender reasonably believes to be done with the intention of wrongfully confining a person, under circumstances which may reasonably cause him to believe that he will be unable to have opportunity for recourse to a public authority for his release.

sub_items.label: (a)
sub_items.text: an assault where the defender reasonably believes that death will otherwise be the consequence of such assault;

sub_items.label: (b)
sub_items.text: an assault where the defender reasonably believes that grievous hurt will otherwise be the consequence of such assault;

sub_items.label: (c)
sub_items.text: an assault that the defender reasonably believes to be done with the intention of committing rape as described in section 375 or causing such rape to be committed;

sub_items.label: (d)
sub_items.text: an assault that the defender reasonably believes to be done with the intention of causing penile penetration of the vagina, anus or mouth as described in section 376(2);

sub_items.label: (e)
sub_items.text: an assault that the defender reasonably believes to be done with the intention of kidnapping or abducting;

sub_items.label: (f)
sub_items.text: an assault that the defender reasonably believes to be done with the intention of wrongfully confining a person, under circumstances which may reasonably cause him to believe that he will be unable to have opportunity for recourse to a public authority for his release.

103.section_title: When such right extends to causing any harm other than death

raw.marginal_note: When such right extends to causing any harm other than death
raw.text: If the offence is not of any of the descriptions mentioned in section 102, the right of private defence of the body does not extend to the voluntary causing of death to the assailant, but does extend, under the restrictions mentioned in sections 98 and 106A, to the voluntary causing to the assailant of any harm other than death.

104.section_title: Commencement and continuance of right of private defence of property

raw.marginal_note: Commencement and continuance of right of private defence of property
raw.text: —(1) The right of private defence of property starts when the defender reasonably believes that there was a danger to property (either his own or that of any other person) arising from any act which is an offence falling under the definition of theft, robbery, mischief or criminal trespass, or which is an attempt to commit such an offence. (2) The right of private defence of property against theft continues till — (3) The right of private defence of property against robbery continues as long as the offender causes or attempts to cause to any person death or hurt or wrongful restraint, or as long as the fear of instant death or of instant hurt or of instant personal restraint continues. (4) The right of private defence of property against criminal trespass or mischief continues as long as the offender continues in the commission of criminal trespass or mischief. (5) The right of private defence of property against house‑breaking continues as long as such house‑breaking continues.

sub_items.text: —(1) The right of private defence of property starts when the defender reasonably believes that there was a danger to property (either his own or that of any other person) arising from any act which is an offence falling under the definition of theft, robbery, mischief or criminal trespass, or which is an attempt to commit such an offence.

(2) The right of private defence of property against theft continues till — ( a ) the offender has effected his retreat with the property; ( b ) the assistance of a public authority is obtained; or ( c ) the property has been recovered.

sub_items.label: (2)(a)
sub_items.text: the offender has effected his retreat with the property;

sub_items.label: (2)(b)
sub_items.text: the assistance of a public authority is obtained; or

sub_items.label: (2)(c)
sub_items.text: the property has been recovered.

(3) The right of private defence of property against robbery continues as long as the offender causes or attempts to cause to any person death or hurt or wrongful restraint, or as long as the fear of instant death or of instant hurt or of instant personal restraint continues.
(4) The right of private defence of property against criminal trespass or mischief continues as long as the offender continues in the commission of criminal trespass or mischief.
(5) The right of private defence of property against house‑breaking continues as long as such house‑breaking continues.

(2) For the purposes of subsection (1)( b ), the 6 ways of entry or leaving by a wrongdoer are as follows:

106.section_title: When such right extends to causing any harm other than death

raw.marginal_note: When such right extends to causing any harm other than death
raw.text: If the offence, the committing or the attempt to commit of which occasions the exercise of the right of private defence, is theft, mischief, or criminal trespass, not of any of the descriptions mentioned in section 105, that right does not extend to the voluntary causing of death, but does extend, subject to the restrictions mentioned in sections 98 and 106A, to the voluntary causing to the wrongdoer of any harm other than death.

106A.section_title: Acts against which there is no right of private defence

raw.marginal_note: Acts against which there is no right of private defence
raw.text: —(1) There is no right of private defence against an act which does not cause the defender to reasonably believe that death or grievous hurt would result, if done, or attempted to be done, by a public servant acting in good faith under the actual or apparent authority of his office, though that act may not be strictly justifiable by law. (2) There is no right of private defence against an act which does not cause the defender to reasonably believe that death or grievous hurt would result, if done, or attempted to be done, by the direction of a public servant acting in good faith under the actual or apparent authority of the public servant’s office, though that direction may not be strictly justifiable by law.

sub_items.text: —(1) There is no right of private defence against an act which does not cause the defender to reasonably believe that death or grievous hurt would result, if done, or attempted to be done, by a public servant acting in good faith under the actual or apparent authority of his office, though that act may not be strictly justifiable by law.

(2) There is no right of private defence against an act which does not cause the defender to reasonably believe that death or grievous hurt would result, if done, or attempted to be done, by the direction of a public servant acting in good faith under the actual or apparent authority of the public servant’s office, though that direction may not be strictly justifiable by law.

Explanation 1.—A person is not deprived of the right of private defence against an act done, or attempted to be done, by a public servant, as such, unless he knows, or has reason to believe, that the person doing the act is such public servant.

Explanation 2.—A person ( A ) is not deprived of the right of private defence against an act done, or attempted to be done, by the direction of a public servant, unless A knows, or has reason to believe, that the person doing the act ( B ) is acting by such direction; or unless B states the authority under which B acts, or, if B has authority in writing, unless B produces such authority, if demanded.

  source: https://sso.agc.gov.sg/Act/PC1871?ProvIds=pr106A-#pr106A-
Effective: 1872-01-01

metadata.summary: Section 106A removes the right of private defence against certain acts by public servants or acts done under their direction when the defender lacks a reasonable belief of death or grievous hurt.

107.section_title: Abetment of the doing of a thing

raw.marginal_note: Abetment of the doing of a thing
raw.text: —(1) A person abets the doing of a thing who — ( a ) instigates any person to do that thing; ( b ) engages with one or more other person or persons in any conspiracy for the doing of that thing, if an act or illegal omission takes place in pursuance of that conspiracy, and in order to the doing of that thing; or ( c ) intentionally aids, by any act or illegal omission, the doing of that thing. (2) A person may abet the doing of a thing despite the existence of facts of which he is unaware which make the doing of the thing impossible.

sub_items.text: —(1) A person abets the doing of a thing who — ( a ) instigates any person to do that thing; ( b ) engages with one or more other person or persons in any conspiracy for the doing of that thing, if an act or illegal omission takes place in pursuance of that conspiracy, and in order to the doing of that thing; or ( c ) intentionally aids, by any act or illegal omission, the doing of that thing.

sub_items.label: (a)
sub_items.text: instigates any person to do that thing;

sub_items.label: (b)
sub_items.text: engages with one or more other person or persons in any conspiracy for the doing of that thing, if an act or illegal omission takes place in pursuance of that conspiracy, and in order to the doing of that thing; or

sub_items.label: (c)
sub_items.text: intentionally aids, by any act or illegal omission, the doing of that thing.
(2) A person may abet the doing of a thing despite the existence of facts of which he is unaware which make the doing of the thing impossible.

Explanation 1.—A person who, by intentional misrepresentation, or by intentional concealment of a material fact which he is bound to disclose, voluntarily causes or procures, or attempts to cause or procure, a thing to be done, is said to instigate the doing of that thing.
Illustration

A , a public officer, is authorised by a warrant from a court of justice to apprehend Z . B , knowing that fact and also that C is not Z , intentionally represents to A that C is Z , and thereby intentionally causes A to apprehend C . Here B abets by instigation the apprehension of C .

Explanation 2.—Whoever, either prior to or at the time of the commission of an act, does anything in order to facilitate the commission of that act, and thereby facilitates the commission thereof, is said to aid the doing of that act.

elements_detail.label: instigation
elements_detail.text: Instigates any person to do that thing

elements_detail.label: conspiracy
elements_detail.text: Engages with one or more other person or persons in any conspiracy for the doing of that thing

elements_detail.label: act_or_illegal_omission_in_pursuance
elements_detail.text: An act or illegal omission takes place in pursuance of that conspiracy

elements_detail.label: in_order_to_do_thing
elements_detail.text: That act or illegal omission is in order to the doing of that thing

elements_detail.label: intentional_aid
elements_detail.text: Intentionally aids, by any act or illegal omission, the doing of that thing

B , knowing that fact and also that C is not Z , intentionally represents to A that C is Z , and thereby intentionally causes A to apprehend C . Here B abets by instigation the apprehension of C .

metadata.summary: Section 107 defines abetment as instigation, conspiracy followed by an act or illegal omission in pursuance, or intentional aid, and states that impossibility unknown to the abettor does not prevent abetment.

raw.marginal_note: Abettor
raw.text: A person abets an offence who abets either the commission of an offence, or the commission of an act which would be an offence, if committed by a person capable by law of committing an offence with the same intention or knowledge as that of the abettor.

sub_items.text: Explanation 1.—The abetment of the illegal omission of an act may amount to an offence, although the abettor may not himself be bound to do that act.
Explanation 2.—To constitute the offence of abetment, it is not necessary that the act abetted should be committed, or that the effect requisite to constitute the offence should be caused.
Illustrations

sub_items.label: a
sub_items.text: ( a ) A instigates B to murder C . B refuses to do so. A is guilty of abetting B to commit murder.

sub_items.label: b
sub_items.text: ( b ) A instigates B to murder D . B , in pursuance of the instigation, stabs D . D recovers from the wound. A is guilty of instigating B to commit murder.

Explanation 3.—It is not necessary that the person abetted should be capable by law of committing an offence, or that he should have the same guilty intention or knowledge as that of the abettor, or any guilty intention or knowledge.
Illustrations

sub_items.label: a
sub_items.text: ( a ) A , with a guilty intention, abets a child or a person of unsound mind to commit an act which would be an offence if committed by a person capable by law of committing an offence, and having the same intention as A . Here A , whether the act is committed or not, is guilty of abetting an offence.

sub_items.label: b
sub_items.text: ( b ) A , with the intention of murdering Z , instigates B , a child below 7 years of age, to do an act which causes Z ’s death. B , in consequence of the abetment, does the act, and thereby causes Z ’s death. Here, though B was not capable by law of committing an offence, A is liable to be punished in the same manner as if B had been capable by law of committing an offence and had committed murder, and he is therefore subject to the punishment of death.

sub_items.label: c
sub_items.text: ( c ) A instigates B to set fire to a dwelling house. B , in consequence of the unsoundness of his mind, being incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that he is doing what is wrong or contrary to law, sets fire to the house in consequence of A ’s instigation. B has committed no offence, but A is guilty of abetting the offence of setting fire to a dwelling house, and is liable to the punishment provided for that offence.

sub_items.label: d
sub_items.text: ( d ) A , intending to cause a theft to be committed, instigates B to take property belonging to Z out of Z ’s possession. A induces B to believe that the property belongs to A . B takes the property out of Z ’s possession, in good faith believing it to be A ’s property. B , acting under this misconception, does not take dishonestly, and therefore does not commit theft. But A is guilty of abetting theft, and is liable to the same punishment as if B had committed theft.
Explanation 4.—The abetment of an offence being an offence, the abetment of such an abetment is also an offence.
Illustration

A instigates B to instigate C to murder Z . B accordingly instigates C to murder Z , and C commits that offence in consequence of B ’s instigation. B is liable to be punished for his offence with the punishment for murder; and as A instigated B to commit the offence A is also liable to the same punishment.

Explanation 5.—It is not necessary to the commission of the offence of abetment by conspiracy that the abettor should concert the offence with the person who commits it. It is sufficient if he engages in the conspiracy in pursuance of which the offence is committed.
Illustration

A concerts with B a plan for poisoning Z . It is agreed that A shall administer the poison. B then explains the plan to C , mentioning that a third person is to administer the poison, but without mentioning A ’s name. C agrees to procure the poison, and procures and delivers it to B for the purpose of its being used in the manner explained. A administers the poison; Z dies in consequence. Here, though A and C have not conspired together, yet C has been engaged in the conspiracy in pursuance of which Z has been murdered. C has therefore committed the offence defined in this section, and is liable to the punishment for murder.

108A.section_title: Abetment in Singapore of an offence outside Singapore

raw.marginal_note: Abetment in Singapore of an offence outside Singapore
raw.text: A person abets an offence within the meaning of this Code who, in Singapore, abets the commission of any act without and beyond Singapore which would constitute an offence if committed in Singapore.

sub_items.text: Illustration
A , in Singapore, instigates B , a foreigner in Java, to commit murder in Java. A is guilty of abetting murder.

elements_detail.label: abetment_in_singapore
elements_detail.text: In Singapore, abets the commission of any act without and beyond Singapore

elements_detail.label: singapore_offence_equivalence
elements_detail.text: That act would constitute an offence if committed in Singapore

108B.section_title: Abetment outside Singapore of an offence in Singapore

raw.marginal_note: Abetment outside Singapore of an offence in Singapore
raw.text: A person abets an offence within the meaning of this Code who abets an offence committed in Singapore notwithstanding that any or all of the acts constituting the abetment were done outside Singapore.

109.section_title: Punishment of abetment if the act abetted is committed in consequence, and where no express provision is made for its punishment

raw.marginal_note: Punishment of abetment if the act abetted is committed in consequence, and where no express provision is made for its punishment
raw.text: Whoever abets any offence shall, if the act abetted is committed in consequence of the abetment, and no express provision is made by this Code for the punishment of such abetment, be punished with the punishment provided for the offence.

sub_items.text: Explanation .—An act or offence is said to be committed in consequence of abetment, when it is committed in consequence of the instigation, or in pursuance of the conspiracy, or with the aid which constitutes the abetment.
Illustrations

sub_items.label: a
sub_items.text: ( a ) A offers a bribe to B , a public servant, as a reward for showing A some favour in the exercise of B ’s official functions. B accepts the bribe. A has abetted the offence defined in section 161.

sub_items.label: b
sub_items.text: ( b ) A instigates B to give false evidence. B , in consequence of the instigation, commits that offence. A is guilty of abetting that offence, and is liable to the same punishment as B .

sub_items.label: c
sub_items.text: ( c ) A and B conspire to poison Z . A , in pursuance of the conspiracy, procures the poison and delivers it to B , in order that he may administer it to Z . B , in pursuance of the conspiracy, administers the poison to Z , in A ’s absence and thereby causes Z ’s death. Here B is guilty of murder. A is guilty of abetting that offence by conspiracy, and is liable to the punishment for murder.

elements_detail.label: abets_offence
elements_detail.text: Abets any offence

elements_detail.label: act_abetted_committed_in_consequence
elements_detail.text: The act abetted is committed in consequence of the abetment

elements_detail.label: no_express_provision_for_punishment
elements_detail.text: No express provision is made by this Code for the punishment of such abetment

110.section_title: Punishment of abetment if the person abetted does the act with a different intention from that of the abettor

raw.marginal_note: Punishment of abetment if the person abetted does the act with a different intention from that of the abettor
raw.text: Whoever abets the commission of an offence shall, if the person abetted does the act with a different intention or knowledge from that of the abettor, be punished with the punishment provided for the offence which would have been committed if the act had been done with the intention or knowledge of the abettor and with no other.

elements_detail.label: abets_commission_of_offence
elements_detail.text: Whoever abets the commission of an offence

elements_detail.label: person_abetted_does_act
elements_detail.text: the person abetted does the act

elements_detail.label: different_intention_or_knowledge
elements_detail.text: with a different intention or knowledge from that of the abettor

  act: Penal Code 1871

Effective: 1872-01-01

Elements of the offence:
  Conjunctive requirement (all_of): every following limb must be proved:
    Physical element (actus reus): Whoever abets the commission of an offence
    Circumstance: the person abetted does the act
    Circumstance: with a different intention or knowledge from that of the abettor

Penalty:
  Penalty to be determined.

  Additionally: be punished with the punishment provided for the offence which would have been committed if the act had been done with the intention or knowledge of the abettor and with no other.

metadata.summary: An abettor is punished according to the offence that would have been committed if the act had been done with the abettor's intention or knowledge alone.

111.section_title: Liability of abettor when one act is abetted and a different act is done

raw.marginal_note: Liability of abettor when one act is abetted and a different act is done
raw.text: When an act is abetted and a different act is done, the abettor is liable for the act done, in the same manner, and to the same extent, as if he had directly abetted it:

sub_items.text: Illustrations

sub_items.label: a
sub_items.text: ( a ) A instigates a child to put poison into the food of Z , and gives him poison for that purpose. The child, in consequence of the instigation, by mistake puts the poison into the food of Y , which is by the side of that of Z . Here, if the child was acting under the influence of A ’s instigation, and the act done was under the circumstances a probable consequence of the abetment, A is liable in the same manner, and to the same extent, as if he had instigated the child to put the poison into the food of Y .

sub_items.label: b
sub_items.text: ( b ) A instigates B to burn Z ’s house. B sets fire to the house, and at the same time commits theft of property there. A , though guilty of abetting the burning of the house, is not guilty of abetting the theft; for the theft was a distinct act, and not a probable consequence of the burning.

sub_items.label: c
sub_items.text: ( c ) A instigates B and C to break into an inhabited house at midnight for the purpose of robbery, and provides them with arms for that purpose. B and C break into the house, and being resisted by Z , one of the inmates, murder Z. Here, if that murder was the probable consequence of the abetment, A is liable to the punishment provided for murder.

elements_detail.label: act_abetted
elements_detail.text: When an act is abetted

elements_detail.label: different_act_done
elements_detail.text: and a different act is done

112.section_title: Abettor, when liable to cumulative punishment for act abetted and for act done

raw.marginal_note: Abettor, when liable to cumulative punishment for act abetted and for act done
raw.text: If the act for which the abettor is liable under section 111 is committed in addition to the act abetted, and constitutes a distinct offence, the abettor is liable to punishment for each of the offences.

sub_items.text: Illustration

A instigates B to resist by force a distress made by a public servant. B , in consequence, resists that distress. In offering the resistance, B voluntarily caused grievous hurt to the officer executing the distress. As B has committed both the offence of resisting the distress, and the offence of voluntarily causing grievous hurt, B is liable to punishment for both these offences; and if A knew that B was likely voluntarily to cause grievous hurt in resisting the distress, A will also be liable to punishment for each of the offences.

elements_detail.label: section_111_liability
elements_detail.text: The abettor is liable under section 111

elements_detail.label: additional_act_committed
elements_detail.text: The act for which the abettor is liable under section 111 is committed in addition to the act abetted

elements_detail.label: distinct_offence
elements_detail.text: That act constitutes a distinct offence

elements_detail.label: cumulative_liability
elements_detail.text: The abettor is liable to punishment for each of the offences

Illustration illustration: A instigates B to resist by force a distress made by a public servant. B , in consequence, resists that distress. In offering the resistance, B voluntarily caused grievous hurt to the officer executing the distress. As B has committed both the offence of resisting the distress, and the offence of voluntarily causing grievous hurt, B is liable to punishment for both these offences; and if A knew that B was likely voluntarily to cause grievous hurt in resisting the distress, A will also be liable to punishment for each of the offences.
  Provenance: statutory illustration attached to Section 112.

outgoing.snippet: The abettor is liable under section 111.

metadata.summary: Section 112 makes an abettor liable to punishment for each offence where the additional act attracting section 111 liability is committed in addition to the act abetted and is a distinct offence.

113.section_title: Liability of abettor for an offence caused by the act abetted different from that intended by the abettor

raw.marginal_note: Liability of abettor for an offence caused by the act abetted different from that intended by the abettor
raw.text: When an act is abetted with the intention on the part of the abettor of causing a particular effect, and an act for which the abettor is liable in consequence of the abetment causes a different effect from that intended by the abettor, the abettor is liable for the effect caused, in the same manner, and to the same extent, as if he had abetted the act with the intention of causing that effect, provided he knew that the act abetted was likely to cause that effect.

sub_items.text: Illustration

A instigates B to cause grievous hurt to Z . B , in consequence of the instigation, causes grievous hurt to Z . Z dies in consequence. Here, if A knew that the grievous hurt abetted was likely to cause death, A is liable to be punished with the punishment provided for murder.

elements_detail.label: act_abetted
elements_detail.text: An act is abetted

elements_detail.label: intention_to_cause_particular_effect
elements_detail.text: The abetment is with the intention on the part of the abettor of causing a particular effect

elements_detail.label: liable_act_causes_different_effect
elements_detail.text: An act for which the abettor is liable in consequence of the abetment causes a different effect from that intended by the abettor

elements_detail.label: liable_for_effect_caused
elements_detail.text: The abettor is liable for the effect caused, in the same manner, and to the same extent, as if he had abetted the act with the intention of causing that effect

elements_detail.label: knowledge_of_likely_effect
elements_detail.text: The abettor knew that the act abetted was likely to cause that effect

115.section_title: Abetment of offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life

raw.marginal_note: Abetment of offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life
raw.text: Whoever abets the commission of an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life, shall, if that offence is not committed in consequence of the abetment, and no express provision is made by this Code or by any other written law for the punishment of such abetment, be punished with imprisonment for a term that may extend to 20 years, and shall also be liable to fine or to caning.

sub_items.text: Illustration

A instigates B to murder Z . The offence is not committed. If B had murdered Z , B would have been subject to the punishment of death. Therefore, A shall be punished with imprisonment for a term that may extend to 20 years, and shall also be liable to fine or to caning.

elements_detail.label: abetment
elements_detail.text: Abets the commission of an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life

elements_detail.label: offence_not_committed

elements_detail.label: no_express_provision
elements_detail.text: No express provision is made by this Code or by any other written law for the punishment of such abetment

metadata.summary: Section 115 punishes abetment of an offence punishable with death or life imprisonment where the offence is not committed and no other law expressly provides the punishment for that abetment.

116.section_title: Abetment of offence punishable with imprisonment

raw.marginal_note: Abetment of offence punishable with imprisonment
raw.text: —(1) Whoever abets an offence punishable with imprisonment shall, if that offence is not committed in consequence of the abetment, and no express provision is made by this Code or any other written law for the punishment of such abetment, be punished in the same manner as if the abettor had committed the offence. (2) Despite subsection (1), where the punishment prescribed for an offence mentioned in that subsection is fixed by law, or is a specified minimum sentence or a mandatory minimum sentence of imprisonment or fine or caning, the court sentencing the person who abetted the offence — (3) To avoid doubt, nothing in subsection (2)( b ) empowers a court to impose a type of punishment that is not prescribed for an offence mentioned in subsection (1) or otherwise provided by any written law for that offence.

sub_items.text: —(1) Whoever abets an offence punishable with imprisonment shall, if that offence is not committed in consequence of the abetment, and no express provision is made by this Code or any other written law for the punishment of such abetment, be punished in the same manner as if the abettor had committed the offence.

(2) Despite subsection (1), where the punishment prescribed for an offence mentioned in that subsection is fixed by law, or is a specified minimum sentence or a mandatory minimum sentence of imprisonment or fine or caning, the court sentencing the person who abetted the offence — ( a ) shall not be bound to impose the fixed, specified minimum or mandatory minimum sentence; and ( b ) may sentence the abettor to such sentence or combination of sentences as the court thinks fit but not exceeding the maximum punishment prescribed for that offence.

sub_items.label: (2)(a)
sub_items.text: shall not be bound to impose the fixed, specified minimum or mandatory minimum sentence; and

sub_items.label: (2)(b)
sub_items.text: may sentence the abettor to such sentence or combination of sentences as the court thinks fit but not exceeding the maximum punishment prescribed for that offence.

(3) To avoid doubt, nothing in subsection (2)( b ) empowers a court to impose a type of punishment that is not prescribed for an offence mentioned in subsection (1) or otherwise provided by any written law for that offence.
Illustrations

sub_items.label: a
sub_items.text: ( a ) A offers a bribe to B , a public servant, as a reward for showing A some favour in the exercise of B ’s official functions. B refuses to accept the bribe. A is punishable under this section for abetting the offence in section 161.

sub_items.label: b
sub_items.text: ( b ) A instigates B to give false evidence. Here, if B does not give false evidence, A has nevertheless committed the offence defined in this section, and is punishable accordingly.

sub_items.label: c
sub_items.text: ( c ) A , a police officer, whose duty it is to prevent robbery, abets the commission of robbery. Here, though the robbery is not committed, A is liable to the maximum term of imprisonment provided for that offence.

sub_items.label: d
sub_items.text: ( d ) B abets the commission of a robbery by A , a police officer, whose duty it is to prevent that offence. Here, though the robbery is not committed, B is liable to the same punishment as if he had committed the offence of robbery.

117.section_title: Abetting the commission of an offence by the public or by more than 10 persons

raw.marginal_note: Abetting the commission of an offence by the public or by more than 10 persons
raw.text: Whoever abets the commission of an offence by the public generally, or by any number or class of persons exceeding 10, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 5 years, or with fine, or with both.

sub_items.text: Illustration

A , an employee at a worksite, affixes a placard at the worksite where more than 10 persons are employed. A instigates the workers to damage property at the worksite if their demand for a pay rise is not met. A has committed an offence under this section.

metadata.summary: This section criminalises abetting an offence to be committed by the public generally or by any number or class of persons exceeding 10.

118.section_title: Concealing a design to commit an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life

raw.marginal_note: Concealing a design to commit an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life
raw.text: Whoever, intending to facilitate, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby facilitate, the commission of an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life, voluntarily conceals, by any act or illegal omission, the existence of a design to commit such offence, or makes any representation which he knows to be false respecting such design, shall, if that offence is committed, be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 7 years, or, if the offence is not committed, with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 3 years: and, in either case, shall also be liable to fine.

sub_items.text: Illustration

A , knowing that a gang-robbery is about to be committed at B , falsely informs the police that a gang‑robbery is about to be committed at C , a place in an opposite direction, and thereby misleads the police with intent to facilitate the commission of the offence. The gang‑robbery is committed at B in pursuance of the design. A is punishable under this section.

119.section_title: A public servant concealing a design to commit an offence which it is his duty to prevent

raw.marginal_note: A public servant concealing a design to commit an offence which it is his duty to prevent
raw.text: Whoever, being a public servant, intending to facilitate, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby facilitate, the commission of an offence, the commission of which it is his duty as such public servant to prevent, voluntarily conceals, by any act or illegal omission, the existence of a design to commit such offence, or makes any representation which he knows to be false respecting such design, shall, if the offence is committed, be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to one‑half of the longest term provided for that offence, or with such fine as is provided for that offence, or with both; or, if the offence is punishable with death or imprisonment for life, be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 15 years, and also be liable to fine; or if the offence is not committed, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to one‑fourth part of the longest term provided for that offence, or with such fine as is provided for that offence, or with both; or, if the offence not committed is punishable with death or imprisonment for life, be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 7 years, and also be liable to fine.

sub_items.text: Illustration

A , an officer of police, being legally bound to give information of all designs to commit robbery which may come to his knowledge, and knowing that B designs to commit robbery, omits to give such information, with intent to facilitate the commission of that offence. Here A has by an illegal omission concealed the existence of B ’s design, and is liable to punishment according to this section.

raw.marginal_note: Definition of criminal conspiracy
raw.text: —(1) When a person agrees with another person to commit an offence or cause an offence to be committed, such an agreement is designated a criminal conspiracy. (2) A person may be a party to a criminal conspiracy despite the existence of facts of which he is unaware which make the commission of the offence impossible. (3) A person may be a party to a criminal conspiracy even though any other person who agrees to commit the offence that is the subject of the conspiracy or to cause that offence to be committed does not intend to carry out that agreement. (4) A person may be a party to a criminal conspiracy in Singapore to commit an offence outside Singapore, which would constitute an offence if committed in Singapore. (5) A person may be a party to a criminal conspiracy to commit an offence in Singapore even though all or any of the acts constituting the criminal conspiracy were done outside Singapore.

sub_items.text: —(1) When a person agrees with another person to commit an offence or cause an offence to be committed, such an agreement is designated a criminal conspiracy.
(2) A person may be a party to a criminal conspiracy despite the existence of facts of which he is unaware which make the commission of the offence impossible.

(3) A person may be a party to a criminal conspiracy even though any other person who agrees to commit the offence that is the subject of the conspiracy or to cause that offence to be committed does not intend to carry out that agreement.
(4) A person may be a party to a criminal conspiracy in Singapore to commit an offence outside Singapore, which would constitute an offence if committed in Singapore.
(5) A person may be a party to a criminal conspiracy to commit an offence in Singapore even though all or any of the acts constituting the criminal conspiracy were done outside Singapore.
Explanation .—It is immaterial whether the offence is the ultimate object of such agreement, or is merely incidental to that object.

elements_detail.label: agreement
elements_detail.text: When a person agrees with another person

elements_detail.label: commit_offence
elements_detail.text: To commit an offence

elements_detail.label: cause_offence_to_be_committed
elements_detail.text: To cause an offence to be committed

metadata.summary: Section 120A defines criminal conspiracy and clarifies when a person may still be a party despite impossibility, lack of intention by another party, or cross-border conduct.

121.section_title: Waging or attempting to wage war or abetting the waging of war against the Government

raw.marginal_note: Waging or attempting to wage war or abetting the waging of war against the Government
raw.text: Whoever wages war against the Government, or attempts to wage such war, or abets the waging of such war, shall be punished with death, or with imprisonment for life.

sub_items.text: Illustration
A joins an insurrection against the Government. A has committed the offence defined in this section.

122.section_title: Collecting arms, etc., with the intention of waging war against the Government

raw.marginal_note: Collecting arms, etc., with the intention of waging war against the Government
raw.text: Whoever collects men, arms or ammunition or otherwise prepares to wage war, with the intention of either waging or being prepared to wage war against the Government, shall be punished with imprisonment for life or imprisonment for a term not exceeding 20 years, and shall, if he is not sentenced to imprisonment for life, also be liable to fine.

123.section_title: Concealing with intent to facilitate a design to wage war

raw.marginal_note: Concealing with intent to facilitate a design to wage war
raw.text: Whoever by any act, or by any illegal omission, conceals the existence of a design to wage war against the Government, intending by such concealment to facilitate, or knowing it to be likely that such concealment will facilitate, the waging of such war, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 15 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

124.section_title: Assaulting President, etc., with intent to compel or restrain the exercise of any lawful power

raw.marginal_note: Assaulting President, etc., with intent to compel or restrain the exercise of any lawful power
raw.text: Whoever, with the intention of inducing or compelling the President or a Member of Parliament or the Cabinet, to exercise or refrain from exercising in any manner any of the lawful powers of the President, or such Member, assaults or wrongfully restrains, or attempts wrongfully to restrain, or overawes by means of criminal force, or the show of criminal force, or attempts so to overawe, the President or such Member, shall be punished with imprisonment for life or for a term which may extend to 20 years, and shall, if he is not sentenced to imprisonment for life, also be liable to fine.

125.section_title: Waging war against any power in alliance or at peace with Singapore

raw.marginal_note: Waging war against any power in alliance or at peace with Singapore
raw.text: Whoever wages war against the government of any power in alliance or at peace with the Government, or attempts to wage such war, or abets the waging of such war, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 15 years, to which fine may be added, or with fine.

126.section_title: Committing depredation on the territories of any power in alliance or at peace with Singapore

raw.marginal_note: Committing depredation on the territories of any power in alliance or at peace with Singapore
raw.text: Whoever commits depredation, or makes preparations to commit depredation, on the territories of any power in alliance or at peace with the Government, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 10 years, and shall also be liable to fine, and any property used, or intended to be used, in committing such depredation, or acquired by such depredation, shall be forfeited.

128.section_title: Public servant voluntarily allowing prisoner of State or war in his custody to escape

raw.marginal_note: Public servant voluntarily allowing prisoner of State or war in his custody to escape
raw.text: Whoever, being a public servant, and having the custody of any prisoner of State or prisoner of war, voluntarily allows such prisoner to escape from any place in which such prisoner is confined, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for a term which may extend to 15 years, and shall, if he is not sentenced to imprisonment for life, also be liable to fine.

130.section_title: Aiding escape of, rescuing, or harbouring such prisoner

raw.marginal_note: Aiding escape of, rescuing, or harbouring such prisoner
raw.text: Whoever knowingly aids or assists any prisoner of State or prisoner of war in escaping from lawful custody or rescues or attempts to rescue any such prisoner, or harbours or conceals any such prisoner who has escaped from lawful custody, or offers or attempts to offer any resistance to the recapture of such prisoner, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 15 years, and shall, if he is not sentenced to imprisonment for life, also be liable to fine.

sub_items.text: Explanation .—A prisoner of State or prisoner of war who is permitted to be at large on his parole within certain limits in Singapore, is said to escape from lawful custody if he goes beyond the limits within which he is allowed to be at large.

elements_detail.label: knowledge
elements_detail.text: Whoever knowingly

elements_detail.label: aiding_or_assisting_escape
elements_detail.text: Aids or assists any prisoner of State or prisoner of war in escaping from lawful custody

elements_detail.label: rescuing_or_attempting_rescue
elements_detail.text: Rescues or attempts to rescue any such prisoner

elements_detail.label: harbouring_or_concealing_escapee
elements_detail.text: Harbours or conceals any such prisoner who has escaped from lawful custody

elements_detail.label: resisting_recapture
elements_detail.text: Offers or attempts to offer any resistance to the recapture of such prisoner

Definitions:
  "explanation_escape_from_lawful_custody" means Explanation .—A prisoner of State or prisoner of war who is permitted to be at large on his parole within certain limits in Singapore, is said to escape from lawful custody if he goes beyond the limits within which he is allowed to be at large.

Elements of the offence:
  Conjunctive requirement (all_of): every following limb must be proved:
    Mental element (mens rea): Whoever knowingly
    Alternative requirement (any_of): proving one following limb is enough:

      Physical element (actus reus): Rescues or attempts to rescue any such prisoner
      Physical element (actus reus): Harbours or conceals any such prisoner who has escaped from lawful custody
      Physical element (actus reus): Offers or attempts to offer any resistance to the recapture of such prisoner

Penalty:
  Shall be punished with imprisonment for a term of not less than no time and not more than 15 years.

  Additionally: or with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 15 years, and shall, if he is not sentenced to imprisonment for life, also be liable to fine.

metadata.summary: Criminalises knowingly helping a prisoner of State or prisoner of war escape, rescuing such a prisoner, harbouring or concealing an escapee, or resisting recapture.

raw.marginal_note: “Harbour”
raw.text: In this Chapter, “harbour” includes the supplying a person with shelter, food, drink, money, clothes, arms, ammunition, or means of conveyance, or the assisting a person in any way to evade apprehension.

130B.section_title: Piracy by law of nations. Cf. 12 and 13 Victoria c. 96 (Admiralty Offences (Colonial) Act 1849)

raw.marginal_note: Piracy by law of nations. Cf. 12 and 13 Victoria c. 96 (Admiralty Offences (Colonial) Act 1849)
raw.text: —(1) A person commits piracy who does any act that, by the law of nations, is piracy. (2) Whoever commits piracy shall be punished with imprisonment for life and with caning with not less than 12 strokes, but if while committing or attempting to commit piracy he murders or attempts to murder another person or does any act that is likely to endanger the life of another person he shall be punished with death.

sub_items.text: —(1) A person commits piracy who does any act that, by the law of nations, is piracy.

(2) Whoever commits piracy shall be punished with imprisonment for life and with caning with not less than 12 strokes, but if while committing or attempting to commit piracy he murders or attempts to murder another person or does any act that is likely to endanger the life of another person he shall be punished with death.

elements_detail.label: aggravated_piracy
elements_detail.text: While committing or attempting to commit piracy, the offender murders or attempts to murder another person or does any act that is likely to endanger the life of another person

metadata.summary: Defines piracy by reference to the law of nations and prescribes life imprisonment with minimum caning, escalating to death for specified life-endangering conduct during piracy.

raw.marginal_note: Piratical acts
raw.text: Whoever, while in or out of Singapore — ( a ) steals a Singapore ship; ( b ) steals or without lawful authority throws overboard, damages or destroys anything that is part of the cargo, supplies or fittings in a Singapore ship; ( c ) does or attempts to do a mutinous act on a Singapore ship; or ( d ) counsels or procures a person to do anything mentioned in paragraph ( a ), ( b ) or ( c ), shall be punished with imprisonment for a term not exceeding 15 years and shall be liable to caning.

sub_items.text: steals a Singapore ship;

sub_items.label: (d)

raw.marginal_note: Genocide
raw.text: A person commits genocide who, with intent to destroy, in whole or in part, a national, an ethnical, a racial or a religious group, commits any of the following acts: ( a ) killing members of the group; ( b ) causing serious bodily or mental harm to members of the group; ( c ) deliberately inflicting on the group conditions of life calculated to bring about its physical destruction in whole or in part; ( d ) imposing measures intended to prevent births within the group; or ( e ) forcibly transferring children of the group to another group.

sub_items.label: (a)
sub_items.text: killing members of the group;

sub_items.label: (b)
sub_items.text: causing serious bodily or mental harm to members of the group;

sub_items.label: (c)
sub_items.text: deliberately inflicting on the group conditions of life calculated to bring about its physical destruction in whole or in part;

sub_items.label: (d)
sub_items.text: imposing measures intended to prevent births within the group; or

sub_items.label: (e)
sub_items.text: forcibly transferring children of the group to another group.

raw.marginal_note: Punishment for genocide
raw.text: Whoever commits genocide shall — ( a ) if the offence consists of the killing of any person, be punished with death; or ( b ) in any other case, be punished with imprisonment for life or with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 20 years.

metadata.summary: Prescribes death where genocide consists of the killing of any person, and otherwise imprisonment for life or up to 20 years.

131.section_title: Abetting mutiny, or attempting to seduce an officer or a serviceman from his duty

raw.marginal_note: Abetting mutiny, or attempting to seduce an officer or a serviceman from his duty
raw.text: Whoever abets the committing of mutiny by an officer or any serviceman in the Singapore Armed Forces or any visiting forces lawfully present in Singapore or attempts to seduce any such officer or serviceman from his allegiance or his duty, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 10 years, and shall, if he is not sentenced to imprisonment for life, also be liable to fine.

elements_detail.label: abets_mutiny
elements_detail.text: Abets the committing of mutiny by an officer or any serviceman in the Singapore Armed Forces or any visiting forces lawfully present in Singapore

elements_detail.label: attempts_to_seduce
elements_detail.text: Attempts to seduce any such officer or serviceman from his allegiance or his duty

132.section_title: Abetment of mutiny, if mutiny is committed in consequence thereof

raw.marginal_note: Abetment of mutiny, if mutiny is committed in consequence thereof
raw.text: Whoever abets the committing of mutiny by an officer or any serviceman in the Singapore Armed Forces or any visiting forces lawfully present in Singapore shall, if mutiny be committed in consequence of that abetment, be punished with death or with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 10 years, and shall, if he is not sentenced to death or imprisonment for life, also be liable to fine.

elements_detail.label: abetment
elements_detail.text: Whoever abets the committing of mutiny by an officer or any serviceman in the Singapore Armed Forces or any visiting forces lawfully present in Singapore

raw.text: Whoever abets the desertion of any officer or any serviceman in the Singapore Armed Forces or any visiting forces lawfully present in Singapore, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 2 years, or with fine, or with both.

raw.marginal_note: Harbouring a deserter

137.section_title: Deserter concealed on board merchant vessel through negligence of master

raw.marginal_note: Deserter concealed on board merchant vessel through negligence of master
raw.text: The master or person in charge of a merchant vessel, on board of which any deserter from the Singapore Armed Forces or any visiting forces lawfully present in Singapore is concealed, shall, though ignorant of such concealment, be punished with a fine not exceeding $1,500, if he might have known of such concealment, but for some neglect of his duty as such master or person in charge, or but for some want of discipline on board of the vessel.

elements_detail.label: concealment_on_board
elements_detail.text: A deserter from the Singapore Armed Forces or any visiting forces lawfully present in Singapore is concealed on board a merchant vessel

elements_detail.text: The accused is ignorant of that concealment

elements_detail.label: neglect_of_duty
elements_detail.text: The accused might have known of that concealment but for some neglect of his duty as such master or person in charge

elements_detail.label: want_of_discipline
elements_detail.text: The accused might have known of that concealment but for some want of discipline on board of the vessel

140.section_title: Wearing the dress of a serviceman

raw.marginal_note: Wearing the dress of a serviceman
raw.text: Whoever, not being a serviceman in the Singapore Armed Forces or any visiting forces lawfully present in Singapore, wears any garb or carries any token resembling any garb or token used by such a serviceman, with the intention that it may be believed that he is such a serviceman, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 6 months, or with fine which may extend to $2,500, or with both.

elements_detail.label: not_serviceman
elements_detail.text: Not being a serviceman in the Singapore Armed Forces or any visiting forces lawfully present in Singapore

elements_detail.label: wear_or_carry
elements_detail.text: Wears any garb or carries any token

elements_detail.label: resemblance
elements_detail.text: The garb or token resembles any garb or token used by such a serviceman

elements_detail.label: intention_to_be_believed
elements_detail.text: With the intention that it may be believed that he is such a serviceman

Effective: 1872-01-01

Elements of the offence:
  Conjunctive requirement (all_of): every following limb must be proved:
    Circumstance: Not being a serviceman in the Singapore Armed Forces or any visiting forces lawfully present in Singapore
    Physical element (actus reus): Wears any garb or carries any token
    Circumstance: The garb or token resembles any garb or token used by such a serviceman
    Mental element (mens rea): With the intention that it may be believed that he is such a serviceman

Penalty:
  Shall be punished with imprisonment for a term of not less than no time and not more than 6 months, or with a fine of not less than S$0.00 and not more than S$2,500.00, or with any combination thereof.

raw.marginal_note: “Harbour”
raw.text: In this Chapter, “harbour” includes the supplying a person with shelter, food, drink, money, clothes, arms, ammunition, or means of conveyance, or the assisting a person in any way to evade apprehension.

140B.section_title: Application of Chapter 7 to Singapore Police Force

raw.marginal_note: Application of Chapter 7 to Singapore Police Force
raw.text: The provisions of this Chapter relating to offences committed in relation to members of the Singapore Armed Forces or any visiting forces lawfully present in Singapore shall apply, with the necessary modifications, to similar acts committed in relation to members of the Singapore Police Force or any volunteer, auxiliary or special force attached to, or coming under the jurisdiction of, that Force.

raw.marginal_note: Unlawful assembly
raw.text: An assembly of 5 or more persons is designated an “unlawful assembly”, if the common object of the persons composing that assembly is — ( a ) to overawe by criminal force, or show of criminal force, the Legislative or Executive Government, or any public servant in the exercise of the lawful power of such public servant; ( b ) to resist the execution of any law, or of any legal process; ( c ) to commit any offence; ( d ) by means of criminal force, or show of criminal force, to any person, to take or obtain possession of any property, or to deprive any person of the enjoyment of a right of way, or of the use of water or other incorporeal right of which he is in possession or enjoyment, or to enforce any right or supposed right; or ( e ) by means of criminal force, or show of criminal force, to compel any person to do what he is not legally bound to do, or to omit to do what he is legally entitled to do.

sub_items.label: (a)
sub_items.text: to overawe by criminal force, or show of criminal force, the Legislative or Executive Government, or any public servant in the exercise of the lawful power of such public servant;

sub_items.label: (b)
sub_items.text: to resist the execution of any law, or of any legal process;

sub_items.label: (c)
sub_items.text: to commit any offence;

sub_items.label: (d)
sub_items.text: by means of criminal force, or show of criminal force, to any person, to take or obtain possession of any property, or to deprive any person of the enjoyment of a right of way, or of the use of water or other incorporeal right of which he is in possession or enjoyment, or to enforce any right or supposed right; or

sub_items.label: (e)
sub_items.text: by means of criminal force, or show of criminal force, to compel any person to do what he is not legally bound to do, or to omit to do what he is legally entitled to do.
Explanation. — An assembly which was not unlawful when it assembled may subsequently become an unlawful assembly.

      Physical element (actus reus): The common object of the persons composing that assembly is by means of criminal force, or show of criminal force, to compel any person to do what he is not legally bound to do, or to omit to do what he is legally entitled to do

142.section_title: Being a member of an unlawful assembly

raw.marginal_note: Being a member of an unlawful assembly
raw.text: Whoever, being aware of facts which render any assembly an unlawful assembly, intentionally joins that assembly, or continues in it, is said to be a member of an unlawful assembly.

metadata.summary: Defines when a person is said to be a member of an unlawful assembly.

raw.marginal_note: Punishment
raw.text: Whoever is a member of an unlawful assembly, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 2 years, or with fine, or with both.

144.section_title: Joining an unlawful assembly armed with any deadly weapon

raw.marginal_note: Joining an unlawful assembly armed with any deadly weapon
raw.text: Whoever, being armed with any deadly weapon, or with anything which, used as a weapon of offence, is likely to cause death, is a member of an unlawful assembly shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 5 years, or with fine, or with caning, or with any combination of such punishments.

sub_items.text: Illustration
A wooden pole sharpened at the end is a thing which, used as a weapon of offence, is likely to cause death.
This illustration is applicable to sections 148 and 158.

elements_detail.label: armed

146.section_title: Force used by one member in prosecution of common object

raw.marginal_note: Force used by one member in prosecution of common object
raw.text: Whenever force or violence is used by an unlawful assembly or by any member thereof, in prosecution of the common object of such assembly, every member of such assembly is guilty of the offence of rioting.

149.section_title: Every member of an unlawful assembly to be deemed guilty of any offence committed in prosecution of common object

raw.marginal_note: Every member of an unlawful assembly to be deemed guilty of any offence committed in prosecution of common object
raw.text: If an offence is committed by any member of an unlawful assembly in prosecution of the common object of that assembly, or such as the members of that assembly knew to be likely to be committed in prosecution of that object, every person who, at the time of the committing of that offence, is a member of the same assembly is guilty of that offence.

150.section_title: Hiring, or conniving at hiring, of persons to join an unlawful assembly

raw.marginal_note: Hiring, or conniving at hiring, of persons to join an unlawful assembly
raw.text: Whoever hires, or engages, or employs, or promotes or connives at the hiring, engagement, or employment of any person to join or become a member of any unlawful assembly shall be punishable as a member of such unlawful assembly, and for any offence which may be committed by any such person as a member of such unlawful assembly, in pursuance of such hiring, engagement, or employment, in the same manner as if he had been a member of such unlawful assembly, or himself had committed such offence.

151.section_title: Knowingly joining or continuing in any assembly of 5 or more persons after it has been commanded to disperse

raw.marginal_note: Knowingly joining or continuing in any assembly of 5 or more persons after it has been commanded to disperse
raw.text: Whoever knowingly joins or continues in any assembly of 5 or more persons likely to cause a disturbance of the public peace, after such assembly has been lawfully commanded to disperse, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 2 years, or with fine, or with both.

sub_items.text: Explanation. — If the assembly is an unlawful assembly within the meaning of section 141, the offender will be punishable under section 145.

152.section_title: Assaulting or obstructing public servant when suppressing riot, etc.

raw.marginal_note: Assaulting or obstructing public servant when suppressing riot, etc.
raw.text: Whoever assaults or threatens to assault, or obstructs or attempts to obstruct, any public servant in the discharge of his duty as such public servant in endeavouring to disperse an unlawful assembly or to suppress a riot or an affray, or uses, or threatens or attempts to use, criminal force to such public servant, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 8 years, or with fine, or with both.

metadata.summary: Assaulting, threatening, obstructing, or using criminal force against a public servant performing duty to disperse an unlawful assembly or suppress a riot or affray is punishable.

153.section_title: Intentionally or rashly giving provocation, with intent to cause riot

raw.marginal_note: Intentionally or rashly giving provocation, with intent to cause riot
raw.text: Whoever intentionally or rashly, by doing anything which is illegal, gives provocation to any person, intending or knowing it to be likely that such provocation will cause the offence of rioting to be committed, shall, if the offence of rioting is committed in consequence of such provocation, be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 3 years, or with fine, or with both; and if the offence of rioting is not committed, with imprisonment for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.

elements_detail.label: intentional
elements_detail.text: Whoever intentionally

elements_detail.label: rash
elements_detail.text: Whoever rashly

elements_detail.label: illegal_act
elements_detail.text: By doing anything which is illegal

elements_detail.label: giving_provocation
elements_detail.text: Gives provocation to any person

elements_detail.label: riot_intent
elements_detail.text: Intending that such provocation will cause the offence of rioting to be committed

elements_detail.label: riot_knowledge
elements_detail.text: Knowing it to be likely that such provocation will cause the offence of rioting to be committed

elements_detail.label: riot_committed
elements_detail.text: The offence of rioting is committed in consequence of such provocation

elements_detail.label: riot_not_committed
elements_detail.text: The offence of rioting is not committed

Elements of the offence:
  Conjunctive requirement (all_of): every following limb must be proved:
    Alternative requirement (any_of): proving one following limb is enough:

metadata.summary: Section 153 criminalises intentionally or rashly giving illegal provocation with intent or knowledge that it is likely to cause rioting, with punishment depending on whether rioting follows.

154.section_title: Owner or occupier of land on which an unlawful assembly is held

raw.marginal_note: Owner or occupier of land on which an unlawful assembly is held
raw.text: Whenever any unlawful assembly or riot takes place, the owner or occupier of the land upon which such unlawful assembly is held or such riot is committed, and any person having or claiming an interest in such land, shall be punishable with fine not exceeding $5,000, if he or his agent or manager, knowing that such offence is being or has been committed, or having reason to believe it is likely to be committed, do not give the earliest notice thereof in his or their power to the principal officer at the nearest police station, and do not, in the case of his or their having reason to believe that it is about to be committed, use all lawful means in his or their power to prevent it, and in the event of its taking place, do not use all lawful means in his or their power to disperse or suppress the riot or unlawful assembly.

elements_detail.label: qualifying_person
elements_detail.text: The person is the owner or occupier of the land upon which such unlawful assembly is held or such riot is committed, or a person having or claiming an interest in such land

elements_detail.label: event
elements_detail.text: An unlawful assembly takes place on that land or a riot is committed on that land

elements_detail.label: knowledge_threshold
elements_detail.text: He or his agent or manager knows that such offence is being or has been committed, or has reason to believe it is likely to be committed

elements_detail.label: no_earliest_notice
elements_detail.text: He or his agent or manager does not give the earliest notice thereof in his or their power to the principal officer at the nearest police station

elements_detail.label: no_prevention
elements_detail.text: He or his agent or manager does not, in the case of his or their having reason to believe that it is about to be committed, use all lawful means in his or their power to prevent it

elements_detail.label: no_dispersal_or_suppression
elements_detail.text: He or his agent or manager does not, in the event of its taking place, use all lawful means in his or their power to disperse or suppress the riot or unlawful assembly

155.section_title: Liability of person for whose benefit a riot is committed

raw.marginal_note: Liability of person for whose benefit a riot is committed
raw.text: Whenever a riot is committed for the benefit or on behalf of any person who is the owner or occupier of any land respecting which such riot takes place, or who claims any interest in such land, or in the subject of any dispute which gave rise to the riot, or who has accepted or derived any benefit therefrom, such person shall be punishable with fine, if he or his agent or manager, having reason to believe that such riot was likely to be committed, or that the unlawful assembly by which such riot was committed was likely to be held, shall not respectively use all lawful means in his or their power to prevent such riot or assembly from taking place, and for suppressing and dispersing the same.

157.section_title: Harbouring persons hired for an unlawful assembly

raw.marginal_note: Harbouring persons hired for an unlawful assembly
raw.text: Whoever harbours, receives or assembles in any house or premises in his occupation or charge, or under his control, any persons, knowing that such persons have been hired, engaged or employed, or are about to be hired, engaged or employed, to join or become members of an unlawful assembly, with the intention of permitting or facilitating them to join or become members of an unlawful assembly, or of screening them or any of them from punishment, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 2 years, or with fine, or with both.

158.section_title: Being hired to take part in an unlawful assembly or riot

raw.marginal_note: Being hired to take part in an unlawful assembly or riot
raw.text: Whoever is engaged or hired, or offers or attempts to be hired or engaged, to do or assist in doing any of the acts specified in section 141, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 2 years, or with fine, or with both; and whoever, being so engaged or hired as aforesaid, goes armed, or engages or offers to go armed, with any deadly weapon, or with anything which used as a weapon of offence is likely to cause death, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 5 years, or with fine, or with both.

sub_items.text: Illustration
The last section is subject to the same illustration as section 144.

161.section_title: Public servant taking a gratification, other than legal remuneration, in respect of an official act

raw.marginal_note: Public servant taking a gratification, other than legal remuneration, in respect of an official act
raw.text: Whoever, being or expecting to be a public servant, accepts or obtains, or agrees to accept or attempts to obtain, from any person, for himself or for any other person, any gratification whatever, other than legal remuneration, as a motive or reward for doing or forbearing to do any official act, or for showing or forbearing to show, in the exercise of his official functions, favour or disfavour to any person, or for rendering or attempting to render any service or disservice to any person, with the Government, or with any Member of Parliament or the Cabinet, or with any public servant, as such, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 3 years, or with fine, or with both.

sub_items.text: Explanations.— “Expecting to be a public servant”. If a person not expecting to be in office obtains a gratification by deceiving others into a belief that he is about to be in office, and that he will then serve them, he may be guilty of cheating, but he is not guilty of the offence defined in this section.
“Gratification”. The word “gratification” is not restricted to pecuniary gratifications, or to gratifications estimable in money.

“Legal remuneration”. The words “legal remuneration” are not restricted to remuneration which a public servant can lawfully demand, but include all remuneration which he is permitted by law to accept.
“A motive or reward for doing”. A person who receives a gratification as a motive for doing what he does not intend to do, or as a reward for doing what he has not done, comes within these words.
Illustrations

sub_items.label: a
sub_items.text: ( a ) A , a judge, obtains from Z , a banker, a situation in Z ’s bank for A ’s brother, as a reward to A for deciding a cause in favour of Z . A has committed the offence defined in this section.

sub_items.label: b
sub_items.text: ( b ) A , a public servant, induces Z erroneously to believe that A ’s influence with another public servant has obtained for Z a contract to do work, and thus induces Z to give A money. A has committed the offence defined in this section.

sub_items.label: c
sub_items.text: ( c ) A , a public servant, induces Z erroneously to believe that A ’s influence with the Government has obtained a grant of land for Z , and thus induces Z to give A money, as a reward for his service. A has committed the offence defined in this section.

If a person not expecting to be in office obtains a gratification by deceiving others into a belief that he is about to be in office, and that he will then serve them, he may be guilty of cheating, but he is not guilty of the offence defined in this section.

  "legal_remuneration" means “Legal remuneration”. The words “legal remuneration” are not restricted to remuneration which a public servant can lawfully demand, but include all remuneration which he is permitted by law to accept.

162.section_title: Taking a gratification in order, by corrupt or illegal means, to influence a public servant

raw.marginal_note: Taking a gratification in order, by corrupt or illegal means, to influence a public servant
raw.text: Whoever accepts or obtains, or agrees to accept or attempts to obtain, from any person, for himself or for any other person, any gratification whatever, as a motive or reward for inducing, by corrupt or illegal means, any public servant to do or to forbear to do any official act, or in the exercise of the official functions of such public servant to show favour or disfavour to any person, or to render or attempt to render any service or disservice to any person, with the Government, or with any Member of Parliament or the Cabinet, or with any public servant, as such, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 3 years, or with fine, or with both.

  Shall be punished with imprisonment for a term of not less than no time and not more than 3 years.

metadata.summary: This section criminalises taking gratification as a motive or reward for influencing a public servant by corrupt or illegal means.

163.section_title: Taking a gratification, for the exercise of personal influence with a public servant

raw.marginal_note: Taking a gratification, for the exercise of personal influence with a public servant
raw.text: Whoever accepts or obtains, or agrees to accept or attempts to obtain, from any person, for himself or for any other person, any gratification whatever, as a motive or reward for inducing, by the exercise of personal influence, any public servant to do or to forbear to do any official act, or in the exercise of the official functions of such public servant to show favour or disfavour to any person, or to render or attempt to render any service or disservice to any person with the Government, or with any Member of Parliament or the Cabinet, or with any public servant, as such, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.

sub_items.text: Illustration

An advocate who receives a fee for arguing a case before a judge; a person who receives pay for arranging and correcting a memorial addressed to Government, setting forth the services and claims of the memorialist; a paid agent for a condemned criminal, who lays before the Government statements tending to show that the condemnation was unjust — are not within this section, inasmuch as they do not exercise or profess to exercise personal influence.

164.section_title: Punishment for abetment by public servant of the offences above defined

raw.marginal_note: Punishment for abetment by public servant of the offences above defined
raw.text: Whoever, being a public servant, in respect of whom either of the offences defined in sections 162 and 163 is committed, abets the offence, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 3 years, or with fine, or with both.

sub_items.text: Illustration

A is a public servant. B , A ’s wife, receives a present as a motive for soliciting A to give an office to a particular person. A abets her doing so. B is punishable with imprisonment for a term not exceeding one year, or with fine, or with both. A is punishable with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 3 years, or with fine, or with both.

elements_detail.text: Whoever, being a public servant

elements_detail.text: In respect of whom either of the offences defined in sections 162 and 163 is committed

elements_detail.text: Abets the offence
