raw.marginal_note: Dishonest misappropriation of property
raw.text: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use movable property, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 2 years, or with fine, or with both.

sub_items.text: Illustrations

sub_items.label: a
sub_items.text: ( a ) A takes property belonging to Z out of Z ’s possession in good faith believing, at the time when he takes it, that the property belongs to himself. A is not guilty of theft; but if A , after discovering his mistake, dishonestly appropriates the property to his own use, he is guilty of an offence under this section.

sub_items.label: b
sub_items.text: ( b ) A , being on friendly terms with Z , goes into Z ’s house in Z ’s absence and takes away a book without Z ’s express consent. Here, if A was under the impression that he had Z ’s implied consent to take the book for the purpose of reading it, A has not committed theft. But if A afterwards sells the book for his own benefit, he is guilty of an offence under this section.

sub_items.label: c
sub_items.text: ( c ) A and B being joint owners of a horse, A takes the horse out of B ’s possession, intending to use it. Here, as A has a right to use the horse, he does not dishonestly misappropriate it. But if A sells the horse and appropriates the whole proceeds to his own use, he is guilty of an offence under this section.
Explanation 1.—A dishonest misappropriation for a time only is a misappropriation within the meaning of this section.
Illustration

A finds a Government promissory note belonging to Z , bearing a blank endorsement. A , knowing that the note belongs to Z , pledges it with a banker as a security for a loan, intending at a future time to restore it to Z . A has committed an offence under this section.

Explanation 2.—A person who finds property not in the possession of any other person, and takes such property for the purpose of protecting it for, or of restoring it to the owner, does not take or misappropriate it dishonestly, and is not guilty of an offence; but he is guilty of the offence above defined, if he appropriates it to his own use, when he knows or has the means of discovering the owner, or before he has used reasonable means to discover and give notice to the owner, and has kept the property a reasonable time to enable the owner to claim it.
What are reasonable means, or what is a reasonable time in such a case, is a question of fact.

It is not necessary that the finder should know who is the owner of the property, or that any particular person is the owner of it; it is sufficient if, at the time of appropriating it, he does not believe it to be his own property, or in good faith believe that the real owner cannot be found.
Illustrations

sub_items.label: a
sub_items.text: ( a ) A finds a dollar on the high road, not knowing to whom the dollar belongs. A picks up the dollar. Here A has not committed the offence defined in this section.

sub_items.label: b
sub_items.text: ( b ) A finds a letter on the high road, containing a bank note. From the direction and contents of the letter he learns to whom the note belongs. He appropriates the note. He is guilty of an offence under this section.

sub_items.label: c
sub_items.text: ( c ) A finds a cheque payable to bearer. He can form no conjecture as to the person who has lost the cheque. But the name of the person who has drawn the cheque appears. A knows that this person can direct him to the person in whose favour the cheque was drawn. A appropriates the cheque without attempting to discover the owner. He is guilty of an offence under this section.

sub_items.label: d
sub_items.text: ( d ) A sees Z drop his purse with money in it. A picks up the purse with the intention of restoring it to Z , but afterwards appropriates it to his own use. A has committed an offence under this section.

sub_items.label: e
sub_items.text: ( e ) A finds a purse with money, not knowing to whom it belongs; he afterwards discovers that it belongs to Z , and appropriates it to his own use. A is guilty of an offence under this section.

sub_items.label: f
sub_items.text: ( f ) A finds a valuable ring, not knowing to whom it belongs. A sells it immediately without attempting to discover the owner. A is guilty of an offence under this section.

sub_items.label: g
sub_items.text: ( g ) A receives money transferred to his bank account from Z ’s bank account. A discovers that Z did not intend to transfer the money to him. A retains the money and appropriates it to his own use. A is guilty of an offence under this section.

elements_detail.text: Property comes into possession of the accused

elements_detail.text: Dishonest intention

elements_detail.label: conversion
elements_detail.text: Converting property to own use

elements_detail.label: disposal
elements_detail.text: Disposing of property contrary to owner's rights

TYPE DEFINITIONS
==================================================

Type "MisappropriationCase" consists of:
  - accused: text

FUNCTIONS

Function "is_misappropriation"(movable (boolean), dishonest (boolean), conversion (boolean)) returning boolean:
  (see decision tree below)

SECTION 403: Dishonest Misappropriation of Property
============================================================
Effective: 1872-01-01

Definitions:
  "misappropriation" means Dishonestly converting to own use property in possession
  "movable_property" means Corporeal property except land and things attached to earth
  "dishonestly" means With intention of causing wrongful gain or wrongful loss
  "conversion" means Dealing with property inconsistent with the rights of the true owner

Elements of the offence:
  Circumstance: Property comes into possession of the accused
  Mental element (mens rea): Dishonest intention
  Physical element (actus reus): Converting property to own use
  Physical element (actus reus): Disposing of property contrary to owner's rights

Penalty:
  Shall be punished with imprisonment for a term of not less than no time and not more than 2 years, or with a fine of not less than S$0.00 and not more than S$10,000.00, or with any combination thereof.

Illustrations:

Illustration example1: A finds a government promissory note. Not knowing who lost it and believing it can be kept, A appropriates it. A may not be guilty as there was no dishonest intent.
  Provenance: statutory illustration attached to Section 403.
Illustration example2: A finds a ring on the highway near B's house. A knows the ring likely belongs to B. A sells the ring without attempting to find the owner. A is guilty of misappropriation.
  Provenance: statutory illustration attached to Section 403.

metadata.summary: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any movable property, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 2 years, or with fine, or with both.

404.section_title: Dishonest misappropriation of property possessed by a deceased person at the time of his death

raw.marginal_note: Dishonest misappropriation of property possessed by a deceased person at the time of his death
raw.text: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use property, knowing that such property was in the possession of a deceased person at the time of that person’s decease, and has not since been in the possession of any person legally entitled to such possession, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 3 years, and shall also be liable to fine; and if the offender at the time of such person’s decease was employed by him as a clerk or servant, the imprisonment may extend to 7 years.

sub_items.text: Illustration

Z dies in possession of furniture and money. His servant A , before the money comes into the possession of any person entitled to such possession, dishonestly misappropriates it. A has committed the offence defined in this section.

elements_detail.text: Whoever dishonestly

elements_detail.label: misappropriation_or_conversion
elements_detail.text: Misappropriates or converts to his own use property

elements_detail.label: knowledge_of_deceased_possession
elements_detail.text: Knowing that such property was in the possession of a deceased person at the time of that person’s decease

elements_detail.label: no_intervening_legal_possession
elements_detail.text: The property has not since been in the possession of any person legally entitled to such possession

============================================================
Jurisdiction: singapore
  act: Penal Code 1871

Elements of the offence:

    Mental element (mens rea): Whoever dishonestly

Penalty:

Illustrations:

metadata.summary: Section 404 criminalises dishonestly misappropriating or converting property known to have been in a deceased person's possession at death before any legally entitled person has taken possession, with a higher imprisonment ceiling for the deceased's clerk or servant.

raw.marginal_note: Criminal breach of trust
raw.text: Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or intentionally suffers any other person to do so, commits “criminal breach of trust”.

sub_items.text: Illustrations

sub_items.label: a
sub_items.text: ( a ) A , being executor to the will of a deceased person, dishonestly disobeys the law which directs him to divide the effects according to the will, and appropriates them to his own use. A has committed criminal breach of trust.

sub_items.label: b
sub_items.text: ( b ) A is a warehouse owner or warehouse operator. Z , going on a journey, entrusts his furniture to A , under a contract that it shall be returned on payment of a stipulated sum for warehouse room. A dishonestly sells the goods. A has committed breach of trust.

sub_items.label: c
sub_items.text: ( c ) A , residing in Singapore, is agent for Z , residing in Penang. There is an express or implied contract between A and Z that all sums remitted by Z to A shall be invested by A according to Z ’s direction. Z remits $5,000 to A , with directions to A to invest the same in Government securities. A dishonestly disobeys the direction, and employs the money in his own business. A has committed criminal breach of trust.

sub_items.label: d
sub_items.text: ( d ) But if A , in the last illustration, not dishonestly, but in good faith, believing that it will be more for Z ’s advantage to hold shares in the Bank X, disobeys Z ’s directions, and buys shares in the Bank X for Z , instead of buying Government securities, here, though Z should suffer loss and should be entitled to bring a civil action against A on account of that loss, yet A , not having acted dishonestly, has not committed criminal breach of trust.

sub_items.label: e
sub_items.text: ( e ) A , a collector of Government money, or a clerk in a Government office, is entrusted with public money, and is either directed by law, or bound by a contract, express or implied, with the Government, to pay into a certain treasury all the public money which he holds. A dishonestly appropriates the money. A has committed criminal breach of trust.

sub_items.label: f
sub_items.text: ( f ) A , a carrier, is entrusted by Z with property to be carried by land or by water. A dishonestly misappropriates the property. A has committed criminal breach of trust.

  "criminal_breach_of_trust" means Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or intentionally suffers any other person to do so, commits “criminal breach of trust”. Illustration illustration_d: ( d ) But if A , in the last illustration, not dishonestly, but in good faith, believing that it will be more for Z ’s advantage to hold shares in the Bank X, disobeys Z ’s directions, and buys shares in the Bank X for Z , instead of buying Government securities, here, though Z should suffer loss and should be entitled to bring a civil action against A on account of that loss, yet A , not having acted dishonestly, has not committed criminal breach of trust.
  Provenance: statutory illustration attached to Section 405.
Illustration illustration_e: ( e ) A , a collector of Government money, or a clerk in a Government office, is entrusted with public money, and is either directed by law, or bound by a contract, express or implied, with the Government, to pay into a certain treasury all the public money which he holds. A dishonestly appropriates the money. A has committed criminal breach of trust.

metadata.summary: Section 405 defines criminal breach of trust as entrusted-property misconduct by dishonest misappropriation, dishonest breach of legal or contractual trust duties, or intentionally suffering another person to do so.

406.section_title: Punishment for Criminal Breach of Trust

raw.marginal_note: Punishment of criminal breach of trust
raw.text: Whoever commits criminal breach of trust shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 7 years, or with fine, or with both.

SECTION 406: Punishment for Criminal Breach of Trust
============================================================
Effective: 1872-01-01

Definitions:
  "criminal_breach_of_trust" means Dishonest misappropriation or conversion of property entrusted to the person
  "entrusted_property" means Property over which a person has dominion by virtue of trust

Elements of the offence:
  Circumstance: Property was entrusted to or placed under dominion of the accused
  Physical element (actus reus): Misappropriation or conversion of the entrusted property
  Mental element (mens rea): Dishonest intention in dealing with the entrusted property

Penalty:
  Shall be punished with imprisonment for a term of not less than no time and not more than 7 years, or with a fine of not less than S$0.00 and not more than S$50,000.00, or with any combination thereof.

Illustrations:

Illustration example1: A, a warehouse keeper, is entrusted with goods by B for safekeeping. A sells the goods and keeps the proceeds. A has committed criminal breach of trust.
  Provenance: statutory illustration attached to Section 406.
Illustration example2: A, a clerk, is entrusted with money by his employer to deposit at the bank. A uses the money for his personal expenses. A has committed criminal breach of trust.
  Provenance: statutory illustration attached to Section 406.

metadata.summary: Whoever commits criminal breach of trust shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 7 years, or with fine, or with both.

407.section_title: Criminal breach of trust of property entrusted for purposes of transportation or storage

raw.marginal_note: Criminal breach of trust of property entrusted for purposes of transportation or storage
raw.text: Whoever, being entrusted with property for the purpose of transportation for hire or storage for rent or charge, commits criminal breach of trust in respect of such property, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 15 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

sub_items.text: Illustration

A carrier is an example of a person who is entrusted with property for the purpose of transportation for hire and a warehouse owner or warehouse operator is an example of a person who is entrusted with property for the purpose of storage for rent or charge.

elements_detail.text: Commits criminal breach of trust in respect of such property

SECTION 407: Criminal breach of trust of property entrusted for purposes of transportation or storage
============================================================
Jurisdiction: singapore
  act: Penal Code 1871
  source: https://sso.agc.gov.sg/Act/PC1871?ProvIds=pr407-#pr407-
Effective: 1872-01-01

Elements of the offence:
  Conjunctive requirement (all_of): every following limb must be proved:
    Alternative requirement (any_of): proving one following limb is enough:
      Circumstance: Being entrusted with property for the purpose of transportation for hire
      Circumstance: Being entrusted with property for the purpose of storage for rent or charge
    Physical element (actus reus): Commits criminal breach of trust in respect of such property

Penalty:
  Shall be punished with imprisonment for a term of not less than no time and not more than 15 years.

  Additionally: Whoever, being entrusted with property for the purpose of transportation for hire or storage for rent or charge, commits criminal breach of trust in respect of such property, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 15 years, and shall also be liable to fine. [15/2019]

Illustrations:

Illustration illustration_1: A carrier is an example of a person who is entrusted with property for the purpose of transportation for hire and a warehouse owner or warehouse operator is an example of a person who is entrusted with property for the purpose of storage for rent or charge.
  Provenance: statutory illustration attached to Section 407.

metadata.summary: Section 407 criminalises committing criminal breach of trust in respect of property entrusted for transportation for hire or storage for rent or charge, with up to 15 years' imprisonment and uncapped fine liability.

408.section_title: Criminal breach of trust by employees

raw.marginal_note: Criminal breach of trust by employees
raw.text: —(1) Whoever, being an employee, and being in any manner entrusted in such capacity with property, or with any dominion over property, commits criminal breach of trust in respect of that property, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 15 years, and shall also be liable to fine. (2) For the purposes of subsection (1) —

sub_items.text: —(1) Whoever, being an employee, and being in any manner entrusted in such capacity with property, or with any dominion over property, commits criminal breach of trust in respect of that property, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 15 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

(2) For the purposes of subsection (1) — ( a ) an employee includes a person who is engaged in a capacity with the same fundamental qualities as an employee; and ( b ) a person may be an employee or engaged in the capacity mentioned in paragraph ( a ) even though that person does not receive any salary or other remuneration arising from the person’s employment or engagement.

sub_items.label: (2)(a)
sub_items.text: an employee includes a person who is engaged in a capacity with the same fundamental qualities as an employee; and

sub_items.label: (2)(b)
sub_items.text: a person may be an employee or engaged in the capacity mentioned in paragraph ( a ) even though that person does not receive any salary or other remuneration arising from the person’s employment or engagement.

elements_detail.label: employee
elements_detail.text: Whoever, being an employee

elements_detail.label: entrusted_in_capacity
elements_detail.text: Being in any manner entrusted in such capacity with property, or with any dominion over property

elements_detail.label: commits_criminal_breach_of_trust
elements_detail.text: Commits criminal breach of trust in respect of that property

metadata.summary: Creates an aggravated criminal breach of trust offence where the offender is an employee or equivalent and is entrusted with property in that capacity.

409.section_title: Criminal breach of trust by public servant, or by banker, merchant, agent, director, officer, partner, key executive or fiduciary

raw.marginal_note: Criminal breach of trust by public servant, or by banker, merchant, agent, director, officer, partner, key executive or fiduciary
raw.text: —(1) Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property — ( a ) in his capacity as a public servant; ( b ) in the way of his trade, profession or business as a banker, a merchant, a factor, a broker, an attorney or an agent; ( c ) in his professional capacity (other than by way of a trade, profession or business mentioned in paragraph ( b )); ( d ) in his capacity as a director of a corporation; ( e ) in his capacity as an officer of an unincorporated association; ( f ) in his capacity as a partner in a partnership; ( g ) in his capacity as a key executive of a corporation, an unincorporated association or a partnership; or ( h ) in his capacity as a fiduciary, commits criminal breach of trust in respect of that property, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 20 years, and shall also be liable to fine. (2) In this section — (3) For the purposes of this section and to avoid doubt —

sub_items.text: —(1) Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property — ( a ) in his capacity as a public servant; ( b ) in the way of his trade, profession or business as a banker, a merchant, a factor, a broker, an attorney or an agent; ( c ) in his professional capacity (other than by way of a trade, profession or business mentioned in paragraph ( b )); ( d ) in his capacity as a director of a corporation; ( e ) in his capacity as an officer of an unincorporated association; ( f ) in his capacity as a partner in a partnership; ( g ) in his capacity as a key executive of a corporation, an unincorporated association or a partnership; or ( h ) in his capacity as a fiduciary, commits criminal breach of trust in respect of that property, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 20 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

sub_items.label: (a)
sub_items.text: in his capacity as a public servant;

sub_items.label: (b)
sub_items.text: in the way of his trade, profession or business as a banker, a merchant, a factor, a broker, an attorney or an agent;

sub_items.label: (c)
sub_items.text: in his professional capacity (other than by way of a trade, profession or business mentioned in paragraph ( b ));

sub_items.label: (d)
sub_items.text: in his capacity as a director of a corporation;

sub_items.label: (e)
sub_items.text: in his capacity as an officer of an unincorporated association;

sub_items.label: (f)
sub_items.text: in his capacity as a partner in a partnership;

sub_items.label: (g)
sub_items.text: in his capacity as a key executive of a corporation, an unincorporated association or a partnership; or

sub_items.label: (h)
sub_items.text: in his capacity as a fiduciary,

(2) In this section — “director” includes — “fiduciary” means a person who has undertaken to act for or on behalf of another person in a matter in circumstances which give rise to a relationship of trust, confidence and loyalty, and includes but is not limited to an executor, an administrator, a liquidator, a receiver and a trustee (other than the trustee of an implied, a constructive or a resulting trust); “key executive” means a person who, whether or not an employee of the corporation, unincorporated association or partnership, and whether acting alone or together with any other person, has general control and management of the administration of that corporation, unincorporated association or partnership; “officer”, in relation to an unincorporated association (other than a partnership), means the president, the secretary or any member of the committee of the unincorporated association, and includes — “partner” includes a person purporting to act as a partner; “partnership” includes a limited partnership and a limited liability partnership within the meaning of section 2(1) of the Limited Liability Partnerships Act 2005.

(3) For the purposes of this section and to avoid doubt — ( a ) a person may be a director of a corporation, officer of an unincorporated association, partner in a partnership, key executive of a corporation, unincorporated association or partnership even though that person does not receive any salary or other remuneration from that corporation, unincorporated association or partnership, as the case may be; and ( b ) a person may be a fiduciary even though it is stated in a contract or an agreement between the parties that a fiduciary relationship does not arise.

sub_items.label: (3)(a)
sub_items.text: a person may be a director of a corporation, officer of an unincorporated association, partner in a partnership, key executive of a corporation, unincorporated association or partnership even though that person does not receive any salary or other remuneration from that corporation, unincorporated association or partnership, as the case may be; and

sub_items.label: (3)(b)
sub_items.text: a person may be a fiduciary even though it is stated in a contract or an agreement between the parties that a fiduciary relationship does not arise.

raw.marginal_note: Stolen property
raw.text: —(1) Property the possession whereof has been transferred by theft, or by extortion, or by robbery, and property which has been criminally misappropriated, or in respect of which criminal breach of trust or cheating has been committed, is designated as “stolen property”, whether the transfer has been made or the misappropriation or breach of trust or cheating has been committed within or without Singapore. But if such property subsequently comes into the possession of a person legally entitled to the possession thereof, it then ceases to be stolen property. (2) The expression “stolen property” includes any property into or for which stolen property has been converted or exchanged and anything acquired by such conversion or exchange, whether immediately or otherwise.

sub_items.text: —(1) Property the possession whereof has been transferred by theft, or by extortion, or by robbery, and property which has been criminally misappropriated, or in respect of which criminal breach of trust or cheating has been committed, is designated as “stolen property”, whether the transfer has been made or the misappropriation or breach of trust or cheating has been committed within or without Singapore. But if such property subsequently comes into the possession of a person legally entitled to the possession thereof, it then ceases to be stolen property.

(2) The expression “stolen property” includes any property into or for which stolen property has been converted or exchanged and anything acquired by such conversion or exchange, whether immediately or otherwise.

Explanation .—For the purpose of proving whether the whole or any part of any property constitutes “stolen property”, it is not necessary for the prosecution to prove the elements or particulars of any offence that caused or contributed to the designation of the property as stolen property.

raw.marginal_note: Receiving stolen property
raw.text: —(1) Whoever receives or retains any property, knowing or having reason to believe the property to be stolen property or property obtained in whole or in part through an offence involving fraud or dishonesty, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 5 years, or with fine, or with both. (2) If the stolen property is a motor vehicle or any component part of a motor vehicle as defined in section 379A(3), a person convicted of an offence under this section — (3) It is a defence for a person charged with an offence under subsection (1) to prove that he has a reasonable excuse to receive or retain the property and that he exercised reasonable care as soon as practicable after having the knowledge or reason to believe mentioned in that subsection.

sub_items.text: —(1) Whoever receives or retains any property, knowing or having reason to believe the property to be stolen property or property obtained in whole or in part through an offence involving fraud or dishonesty, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 5 years, or with fine, or with both.

(2) If the stolen property is a motor vehicle or any component part of a motor vehicle as defined in section 379A(3), a person convicted of an offence under this section — ( a ) shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 5 years, and shall also be liable to fine; and ( b ) may be disqualified for such period as the court may order from the date of his release from imprisonment from holding or obtaining a driving licence under the Road Traffic Act 1961.

sub_items.label: (2)(a)
sub_items.text: shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 5 years, and shall also be liable to fine; and

sub_items.label: (2)(b)
sub_items.text: may be disqualified for such period as the court may order from the date of his release from imprisonment from holding or obtaining a driving licence under the Road Traffic Act 1961.

(3) It is a defence for a person charged with an offence under subsection (1) to prove that he has a reasonable excuse to receive or retain the property and that he exercised reasonable care as soon as practicable after having the knowledge or reason to believe mentioned in that subsection.
Illustration

A large sum of money is credited into A ’s bank account through a fund transfer. A later discovers the funds on checking his bank statement and has reason to believe that the funds were obtained in whole or in part through a criminal offence involving fraud or dishonesty. As soon as practicable after this discovery, A makes a report of the funds to the bank management and expresses his belief of the origins of the funds. A has a reasonable excuse for receiving and retaining the funds in his bank account while the report is being processed and has exercised reasonable care.

SECTION 411: Dishonestly Receiving Stolen Property
============================================================
Effective: 1872-01-01

Definitions:
  "stolen_property" means Property the possession of which has been transferred by theft, extortion, robbery, or criminal misappropriation
  "dishonestly_receiving" means Receiving or retaining property knowing or having reason to believe it to be stolen

Elements of the offence:
  Circumstance: Property is stolen property as defined
  Mental element (mens rea): Knowing or having reason to believe the property to be stolen
  Physical element (actus reus): Dishonestly receiving or retaining the stolen property

Penalty:
  Shall be punished with imprisonment for a term of not less than no time and not more than 5 years, or with a fine of not less than S$0.00 and not more than S$20,000.00, or with any combination thereof.

Illustrations:

Illustration example1: A buys a laptop from B at a suspiciously low price, knowing that B likely stole it. A has dishonestly received stolen property.
  Provenance: statutory illustration attached to Section 411.
Illustration example2: A finds goods left at her doorstep. A has no reason to believe they are stolen and keeps them. A has not committed the offence.
  Provenance: statutory illustration attached to Section 411.

metadata.summary: Whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen property, knowing or having reason to believe the property to be stolen, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 5 years, or with fine, or with both.

412.section_title: Receiving property stolen in the commission of a gang-robbery

raw.marginal_note: Receiving property stolen in the commission of a gang-robbery
raw.text: —(1) Whoever receives or retains any stolen property, the possession whereof he knows or has reason to believe to have been transferred by the commission of gang‑robbery, or receives from a person, whom he knows or has reason to believe to belong or to have belonged to gang‑robbers, property which he knows or has reason to believe to have been stolen, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 20 years, and shall also be liable to fine. (2) It is a defence for a person charged with an offence under subsection (1) to prove that he has a reasonable excuse to receive or retain the property and that he exercised reasonable care as soon as practicable after having the knowledge or reason to believe mentioned in that subsection.

sub_items.text: —(1) Whoever receives or retains any stolen property, the possession whereof he knows or has reason to believe to have been transferred by the commission of gang‑robbery, or receives from a person, whom he knows or has reason to believe to belong or to have belonged to gang‑robbers, property which he knows or has reason to believe to have been stolen, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 20 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

(2) It is a defence for a person charged with an offence under subsection (1) to prove that he has a reasonable excuse to receive or retain the property and that he exercised reasonable care as soon as practicable after having the knowledge or reason to believe mentioned in that subsection.
Illustration

A box of valuable jewels is delivered by courier to A ’s office. A later discovers the jewels and learns from the news reports that there was a gang robbery of jewels in the vicinity. A has reason to believe that the jewels were stolen in the gang robbery. As soon as practicable after this discovery, A keeps the jewels in the safe and travels to the police station to make a report of the jewels and express his belief that the jewels were stolen by gang‑robbers. A has a reasonable excuse for receiving and retaining the jewels pending their retrieval by the police and has exercised reasonable care.

raw.marginal_note: Cheating
raw.text: Whoever, by deceiving any person, whether or not such deception was the sole or main inducement, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person so deceived to deliver or cause the delivery of any property to any person, or to consent that any person shall retain any property, or intentionally induces the person so deceived to do or omit to do anything which he would not do or omit to do if he were not so deceived, and which act or omission causes or is likely to cause damage or harm to any person in body, mind, reputation or property, is said to “cheat”.

sub_items.text: Explanation 1.—A dishonest concealment of facts is a deception within the meaning of this section.
Explanation 2.—Mere breach of contract is not of itself proof of an original fraudulent intent.
Explanation 3.—Whoever makes a representation through any agent is to be treated as having made the representation himself.

Explanation 4.—A person that is a company or association or body of persons, whether incorporated or not, can be deceived for the purposes of this section, even though none of its individual officers, employees or agents is personally deceived.

Explanation 5.—A person that is a company or association or body of persons, whether incorporated or not, can be induced to act in a manner mentioned in this section even though none of its individual officers, employees or agents is personally induced to act in such manner.
Illustrations

sub_items.label: a
sub_items.text: ( a ) A , by falsely pretending to be in the Government service, intentionally deceives Z , and thus dishonestly induces Z to let him have on credit goods for which he does not mean to pay. A cheats.

sub_items.label: b
sub_items.text: ( b ) A , by putting a counterfeit mark on an article, intentionally deceives Z into a belief that this article was made by a certain celebrated manufacturer, and thus dishonestly induces Z to buy and pay for the article. A cheats.

sub_items.label: c
sub_items.text: ( c ) A , by exhibiting to Z a false sample of an article, intentionally deceives Z into believing that the article corresponds with the sample, and thereby dishonestly induces Z to buy and pay for the article. A cheats.

sub_items.label: d
sub_items.text: ( d ) A , by tendering in payment for an article a bill on a house with which A keeps no money, and by which A expects that the bill will be dishonoured, intentionally deceives Z , and thereby dishonestly induces Z to deliver the article, intending not to pay for it. A cheats.

sub_items.label: e
sub_items.text: ( e ) A , by pledging as diamonds articles which he knows are not diamonds, intentionally deceives Z , and thereby dishonestly induces Z to lend money. A cheats.

sub_items.label: f
sub_items.text: ( f ) A intentionally deceives Z into a belief that A means to repay any money that Z may lend to him, and thereby dishonestly induces Z to lend him money, A not intending to repay it. A cheats.

sub_items.label: g
sub_items.text: ( g ) A intentionally deceives Z into a belief that A means to deliver to Z a certain quantity of pepper which he does not intend to deliver, and thereby dishonestly induces Z to advance money upon the faith of such delivery. A cheats; but if A , at the time of obtaining the money, intends to deliver the pepper, and afterwards breaks his contract and does not deliver it, he does not cheat, but is liable only to a civil action for breach of contract.

sub_items.label: h
sub_items.text: ( h ) A intentionally deceives Z into a belief that A has performed A ’s part of a contract made with Z , which he has not performed, and thereby dishonestly induces Z to pay money. A cheats.

sub_items.label: i
sub_items.text: ( i ) A sells and conveys an estate to B . A , knowing that in consequence of such sale he has no right to the property, sells or mortgages the same to Z without disclosing the fact of the previous sale and conveyance to B , and receives the purchase or mortgage money from Z . A cheats.

sub_items.label: j
sub_items.text: ( j ) A , playing with false dice, or marked cards, wins money from B . A cheats.

sub_items.label: k
sub_items.text: ( k ) A places an order for concert tickets in the automated concert ticketing system of a company, Z , using stolen credit card details, and thereby causes Z ’s ticketing system to electronically deliver the electronic concert tickets to A . Z has been deceived and induced into delivering the tickets to A even though no human officer, employee or agent of Z has been personally deceived or induced.

SECTION 415: Cheating
============================================================
Effective: 1872-01-01

Definitions:
  "deceive" means To cause a person to believe something that is false
  "fraudulently" means With intent to defraud another person
  "dishonestly" means With intention of causing wrongful gain or wrongful loss
  "property" means Any movable or immovable property
  "inducement" means Causing a person to act based on deception

Elements of the offence:
  Conjunctive requirement (all_of): every following limb must be proved:
    Physical element (actus reus): Deceiving any person
    Alternative requirement (any_of): proving one following limb is enough:
      Mental element (mens rea): Fraudulently inducing the person
      Mental element (mens rea): Dishonestly inducing the person
    Physical element (actus reus): Inducing delivery of property, consent to retain property, or act/omission
    Circumstance: Causing or likely to cause damage to body, mind, reputation, or property

Penalty:
  Shall be punished with imprisonment for a term of not less than 1 year and not more than 7 years, or with a fine of not less than S$0.00 and not more than S$50,000.00, or with any combination thereof.

Illustrations:

Illustration example1: A intentionally deceives B into believing that a worthless article is valuable, and thus induces B to buy it. A cheats.
  Provenance: statutory illustration attached to Section 415.
Illustration example2: A falsely pretends to be in government service and induces B to let him have goods on credit. A cheats.
  Provenance: statutory illustration attached to Section 415.
Illustration example3: A deceives B into believing that A means to repay a loan, inducing B to lend money which A does not intend to repay. A cheats.
  Provenance: statutory illustration attached to Section 415.

per s24: intent to cause wrongful gain/loss

metadata.summary: Whoever, by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person so deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to consent that any person shall retain any property, or intentionally induces the person so deceived to do or omit to do anything which he would not do or omit if he were not so deceived, and which act or omission causes or is likely to cause damage or harm to that person in body, mind, reputation or property, is said to 'cheat'.

raw.marginal_note: Cheating by personation
raw.text: A person is said to “cheat by personation”, if he cheats by pretending to be some other person, or by knowingly substituting one person for another, or representing that he or any other person is a person other than he or such other person really is.

sub_items.text: Explanation.— The offence is committed whether the individual personated is a real or an imaginary person.
Illustrations

sub_items.label: a
sub_items.text: ( a ) A cheats by pretending to be a certain rich banker of the same name. A cheats by personation.

sub_items.label: b
sub_items.text: ( b ) A cheats by pretending to be B , a person who is deceased. A cheats by personation.

raw.marginal_note: Illegally obtained personal information
raw.text: —(1) A person ( A ) shall be guilty of an offence who, knowing or having reason to believe that any personal information about another person ( B ) (being an individual) was obtained without B ’s consent — ( a ) obtains or retains the personal information; or ( b ) supplies, offers to supply, transmits or makes available, by any means, the personal information. (2) It is not an offence under subsection (1)( a ) if the person obtained or retained the personal information for a purpose other than — (3) It is not an offence under subsection (1)( b ) if — (4) For the purposes of subsection (1)( b ), a person does not transmit or make available personal information merely because the person provides, or operates facilities for network access, or provides services relating to, or provides connections for, the transmission or routing of data. (5) A person who is guilty of an offence under subsection (1) shall on conviction be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 3 years, or with fine which may extend to $10,000, or with both. (6) For the purpose of proving under subsection (1) that a person knows or has reason to believe that any personal information of an individual ( B ) was obtained without B ’s consent, it is not necessary for the prosecution to prove the particulars of the obtaining of the personal information, such as who obtained the information and when it took place. (7) In this section —

sub_items.text: —(1) A person ( A ) shall be guilty of an offence who, knowing or having reason to believe that any personal information about another person ( B ) (being an individual) was obtained without B ’s consent — ( a ) obtains or retains the personal information; or ( b ) supplies, offers to supply, transmits or makes available, by any means, the personal information.

sub_items.label: (a)
sub_items.text: obtains or retains the personal information; or

sub_items.label: (b)
sub_items.text: supplies, offers to supply, transmits or makes available, by any means, the personal information.

(2) It is not an offence under subsection (1)( a ) if the person obtained or retained the personal information for a purpose other than — ( a ) for use in committing, or in facilitating the commission of, any offence under this Code or any other written law; or ( b ) for supply, transmission or making available by any means for the personal information to be used in committing, or in facilitating the commission of, any offence under this Code or any other written law.

sub_items.label: (2)(a)
sub_items.text: for use in committing, or in facilitating the commission of, any offence under this Code or any other written law; or

sub_items.label: (2)(b)

(3) It is not an offence under subsection (1)( b ) if — ( a ) the person did the act for a purpose other than for the personal information to be used in committing, or in facilitating the commission of, any offence under this Code or any other written law; and ( b ) the person did not know or have reason to believe that the personal information will be or is likely to be used to commit, or facilitate the commission of, any offence under this Code or any other written law.

sub_items.label: (3)(a)
sub_items.text: the person did the act for a purpose other than for the personal information to be used in committing, or in facilitating the commission of, any offence under this Code or any other written law; and

sub_items.label: (3)(b)

(4) For the purposes of subsection (1)( b ), a person does not transmit or make available personal information merely because the person provides, or operates facilities for network access, or provides services relating to, or provides connections for, the transmission or routing of data.

(5) A person who is guilty of an offence under subsection (1) shall on conviction be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 3 years, or with fine which may extend to $10,000, or with both.

(6) For the purpose of proving under subsection (1) that a person knows or has reason to believe that any personal information of an individual ( B ) was obtained without B ’s consent, it is not necessary for the prosecution to prove the particulars of the obtaining of the personal information, such as who obtained the information and when it took place.

(7) In this section — ( a ) personal information is any information, whether true or not, about an individual of a type that is commonly used alone or in combination with other information to identify or purport to identify an individual, including (but not limited to) biometric data, name, address, date of birth, national registration identity card number, passport number, a written, electronic or digital signature, user authentication code, credit card or debit card number, and password; and ( b ) a reference to an offence under this Code includes an offence under subsection (1).

sub_items.label: (7)(b)
sub_items.text: a reference to an offence under this Code includes an offence under subsection (1).

420.section_title: Cheating and dishonestly inducing a delivery of property

raw.marginal_note: Cheating and dishonestly inducing a delivery of property
raw.text: —(1) Whoever cheats and thereby dishonestly induces the person deceived to deliver or cause the delivery of any property to any person, or to make, alter or destroy the whole or any part of a valuable security, or anything which is signed or sealed, and which is capable of being converted into a valuable security, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 10 years, and shall also be liable to fine or to caning or to both. (2) Whoever cheats by remote communication and thereby dishonestly induces any of the acts mentioned in subsection (1) shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 10 years and with caning with not less than 6 strokes, and shall also be liable to fine.

sub_items.text: —(1) Whoever cheats and thereby dishonestly induces the person deceived to deliver or cause the delivery of any property to any person, or to make, alter or destroy the whole or any part of a valuable security, or anything which is signed or sealed, and which is capable of being converted into a valuable security, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 10 years, and shall also be liable to fine or to caning or to both.

(2) Whoever cheats by remote communication and thereby dishonestly induces any of the acts mentioned in subsection (1) shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 10 years and with caning with not less than 6 strokes, and shall also be liable to fine.

raw.marginal_note: Obtaining services dishonestly or fraudulently
raw.text: —(1) A person shall be guilty of an offence if he obtains services for himself or another person dishonestly or fraudulently and — ( a ) the services are made available on the basis that payment has been, is being or will be made for or in respect of them; ( b ) the person obtains the services without any payment having been made for or in respect of them or without payment having been made in full; and ( c ) when the person obtains the services — (2) A person who is guilty of an offence under subsection (1) shall on conviction be liable to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 10 years, or to fine, or to both.

sub_items.text: —(1) A person shall be guilty of an offence if he obtains services for himself or another person dishonestly or fraudulently and — ( a ) the services are made available on the basis that payment has been, is being or will be made for or in respect of them; ( b ) the person obtains the services without any payment having been made for or in respect of them or without payment having been made in full; and ( c ) when the person obtains the services —

sub_items.label: (a)
sub_items.text: the services are made available on the basis that payment has been, is being or will be made for or in respect of them;

sub_items.label: (b)
sub_items.text: the person obtains the services without any payment having been made for or in respect of them or without payment having been made in full; and

sub_items.label: (c)
sub_items.text: when the person obtains the services —

sub_items.label: (c)(i)
sub_items.text: the person knows that they are being made available on the basis mentioned in paragraph ( a ) or that they might be; and

sub_items.label: (c)(ii)
sub_items.text: the person intends that payment will not be made or will not be made in full.
(2) A person who is guilty of an offence under subsection (1) shall on conviction be liable to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 10 years, or to fine, or to both.
Explanation .—The services obtained are not excluded from this section merely because such services also involve the provision of goods or other things.
Illustration

A obtains services in the form of air transportation on an airline from Singapore to an overseas destination. A is also served food and beverages on the airline. A has obtained services even though A has also been provided with food and beverages.

424A.section_title: Fraud by false representation, non-disclosure or abuse of position not connected with contracts for goods or services

raw.marginal_note: Fraud by false representation, non-disclosure or abuse of position not connected with contracts for goods or services
raw.text: —(1) A person shall be guilty of an offence if he, fraudulently or dishonestly — ( a ) makes a false representation; ( b ) fails to disclose to another person information which he is under a legal duty to disclose; or ( c ) abuses, whether by act or omission, a position which he occupies in which he is expected to safeguard, or not to act against, the financial interests of another person. (2) A person may be guilty of an offence under subsection (1) whether or not the acts in subsection (1)( a ), ( b ) or ( c ) were material. (3) A person who is guilty of an offence under subsection (1) shall on conviction be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 20 years, or with fine, or with both. (4) This section does not apply where the false representation, failure to disclose or abuse of position mentioned in subsection (1) is directly connected with a written or oral contract for the supply of goods or services. (5) For the purposes of this section and section 424B —

sub_items.text: —(1) A person shall be guilty of an offence if he, fraudulently or dishonestly — ( a ) makes a false representation; ( b ) fails to disclose to another person information which he is under a legal duty to disclose; or ( c ) abuses, whether by act or omission, a position which he occupies in which he is expected to safeguard, or not to act against, the financial interests of another person.

sub_items.label: (a)
sub_items.text: makes a false representation;

sub_items.label: (b)
sub_items.text: fails to disclose to another person information which he is under a legal duty to disclose; or

sub_items.label: (c)
sub_items.text: abuses, whether by act or omission, a position which he occupies in which he is expected to safeguard, or not to act against, the financial interests of another person.
(2) A person may be guilty of an offence under subsection (1) whether or not the acts in subsection (1)( a ), ( b ) or ( c ) were material.
(3) A person who is guilty of an offence under subsection (1) shall on conviction be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 20 years, or with fine, or with both.

(4) This section does not apply where the false representation, failure to disclose or abuse of position mentioned in subsection (1) is directly connected with a written or oral contract for the supply of goods or services.

(5) For the purposes of this section and section 424B — ( a ) “representation” means any representation as to fact or law, including a representation as to the state of mind of the person making the representation, or any other person; ( b ) a person makes a false representation if it is untrue or misleading, and that person knows that it is, or might be, untrue or misleading; ( c ) a representation may be express or implied; and ( d ) a representation may be regarded as made if it (or anything implying it) is submitted in any form to any system or device designed to receive, convey or respond to communications (with or without human intervention).

sub_items.label: (5)(a)
sub_items.text: “representation” means any representation as to fact or law, including a representation as to the state of mind of the person making the representation, or any other person;

sub_items.label: (5)(b)
sub_items.text: a person makes a false representation if it is untrue or misleading, and that person knows that it is, or might be, untrue or misleading;

sub_items.label: (5)(c)
sub_items.text: a representation may be express or implied; and

sub_items.label: (5)(d)
sub_items.text: a representation may be regarded as made if it (or anything implying it) is submitted in any form to any system or device designed to receive, convey or respond to communications (with or without human intervention).

424B.section_title: Fraud by false representation, non-disclosure or abuse of position

raw.marginal_note: Fraud by false representation, non-disclosure or abuse of position
raw.text: —(1) A person shall be guilty of an offence if he, fraudulently or dishonestly — ( a ) makes a false representation; ( b ) fails to disclose to another person information which he is under a legal duty to disclose; or ( c ) abuses, whether by act or omission, a position which he occupies in which he is expected to safeguard, or not to act against, the financial interests of another person. (2) A person may be guilty of an offence under subsection (1) whether or not the acts in subsection (1)( a ), ( b ) or ( c ) were material. (3) A person who is guilty of an offence under subsection (1) shall on conviction be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 20 years, or with fine, or with both. (4) This section does not apply to any act mentioned in subsection (1) which would constitute an offence under section 424A.

sub_items.text: —(1) A person shall be guilty of an offence if he, fraudulently or dishonestly — ( a ) makes a false representation; ( b ) fails to disclose to another person information which he is under a legal duty to disclose; or ( c ) abuses, whether by act or omission, a position which he occupies in which he is expected to safeguard, or not to act against, the financial interests of another person.

sub_items.text: abuses, whether by act or omission, a position which he occupies in which he is expected to safeguard, or not to act against, the financial interests of another person.
(2) A person may be guilty of an offence under subsection (1) whether or not the acts in subsection (1)( a ), ( b ) or ( c ) were material.
(3) A person who is guilty of an offence under subsection (1) shall on conviction be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 20 years, or with fine, or with both.
(4) This section does not apply to any act mentioned in subsection (1) which would constitute an offence under section 424A.

raw.marginal_note: Mischief
raw.text: Whoever, with intent to cause, or knowing that he is likely to cause, wrongful loss or damage to the public or any person, causes the destruction of any property, or any such change in any property, or in the situation thereof, as destroys or diminishes its value or utility, or affects it injuriously, commits “mischief”.

sub_items.text: Explanation 1.—It is not essential to the offence of mischief that the offender should intend to cause loss or damage to the owner of the property injured or destroyed. It is sufficient if he intends to cause, or knows that he is likely to cause, wrongful loss or damage to any person by injuring any property, whether it belongs to that person or not.
Explanation 2.—Mischief may be committed by an act affecting property belonging to the person who commits the act, or to that person and others jointly.
Illustrations

sub_items.label: a
sub_items.text: ( a ) A voluntarily burns a valuable security belonging to Z , intending to cause wrongful loss to Z . A has committed mischief.

sub_items.label: b
sub_items.text: ( b ) [ Deleted by Act 51 of 2007 ]

sub_items.label: c
sub_items.text: ( c ) A voluntarily throws into a river a ring belonging to Z , with the intention of thereby causing wrongful loss to Z . A has committed mischief.

sub_items.label: d
sub_items.text: ( d ) A , knowing that his effects are about to be taken in execution, or under or pursuant to an enforcement order, in order to satisfy a debt due from him to Z , destroys those effects, with the intention of thereby preventing Z from obtaining satisfaction of the debt, and of thus causing damage to Z . A has committed mischief.

sub_items.label: e
sub_items.text: ( e ) A , having insured a ship, voluntarily causes the same to be cast away, with the intention of causing damage to the underwriters. A has committed mischief.

sub_items.label: f
sub_items.text: ( f ) A causes a ship to be cast away, intending thereby to cause damage to Z , who has lent money on bottomry on the ship. A has committed mischief.

sub_items.label: g
sub_items.text: ( g ) A , having joint property with Z in a horse, shoots the horse, intending thereby to cause wrongful loss to Z . A has committed mischief.

elements_detail.label: intent_to_cause_wrongful_loss_or_damage
elements_detail.text: With intent to cause wrongful loss or damage to the public or any person

elements_detail.label: knowledge_of_likely_wrongful_loss_or_damage
elements_detail.text: Knowing that he is likely to cause wrongful loss or damage to the public or any person

elements_detail.label: destruction_of_property
elements_detail.text: Causes the destruction of any property

elements_detail.label: change_in_property
elements_detail.text: Causes any such change in any property, or in the situation thereof, as destroys or diminishes its value or utility, or affects it injuriously

SECTION 425: Mischief
============================================================
Jurisdiction: singapore

  source: https://sso.agc.gov.sg/Act/PC1871?ProvIds=pr425-#pr425-
Effective: 1872-01-01

Definitions:
  "explanation_1" means Explanation 1.—It is not essential to the offence of mischief that the offender should intend to cause loss or damage to the owner of the property injured or destroyed. It is sufficient if he intends to cause, or knows that he is likely to cause, wrongful loss or damage to any person by injuring any property, whether it belongs to that person or not.
  "explanation_2" means Explanation 2.—Mischief may be committed by an act affecting property belonging to the person who commits the act, or to that person and others jointly.

Elements of the offence:
  Conjunctive requirement (all_of): every following limb must be proved:
    Alternative requirement (any_of): proving one following limb is enough:

      Mental element (mens rea): Knowing that he is likely to cause wrongful loss or damage to the public or any person
    Alternative requirement (any_of): proving one following limb is enough:
      Physical element (actus reus): Causes the destruction of any property
      Physical element (actus reus): Causes any such change in any property, or in the situation thereof, as destroys or diminishes its value or utility, or affects it injuriously

A has committed mischief.
  Provenance: statutory illustration attached to Section 425.
Illustration illustration_e: ( e ) A , having insured a ship, voluntarily causes the same to be cast away, with the intention of causing damage to the underwriters. A has committed mischief.

427.section_title: Punishment for committing mischief causing disruption to key service, etc.

raw.marginal_note: Punishment for committing mischief causing disruption to key service, etc.
raw.text: —(1) Whoever commits mischief by doing any act which causes, or which he knows is likely to cause, a disruption to — ( a ) the provision of any key service; or ( b ) the performance of any duty or function of, or the exercise of any power by the Government or a public agency, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 10 years, or with fine, or with both. (2) In subsection (1) —

sub_items.text: —(1) Whoever commits mischief by doing any act which causes, or which he knows is likely to cause, a disruption to — ( a ) the provision of any key service; or ( b ) the performance of any duty or function of, or the exercise of any power by the Government or a public agency, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 10 years, or with fine, or with both.

sub_items.label: (a)
sub_items.text: the provision of any key service; or

sub_items.label: (b)
sub_items.text: the performance of any duty or function of, or the exercise of any power by the Government or a public agency,
(2) In subsection (1) — “key service” means any of the following services: “public agency” means —
Illustrations

sub_items.label: a
sub_items.text: ( a ) An act which causes damage to enforcement cameras installed in a public place by a statutory board responsible for land transport to detect illegal parking in a public road is an act which causes disruption to the performance of the function of that statutory board.

sub_items.label: b
sub_items.text: ( b ) Unauthorised destruction of the records and files of a department of the Government which role is to promote Singapore as a sound and reputable financial centre is an act which causes disruption to the performance of the function of that department.

sub_items.label: c
sub_items.text: ( c ) An act of mischief which damages an electricity licensee’s power generation facility that is used to supply electricity to a public housing estate, is an act which is likely to cause disruption to a key service even though there is no actual disruption to that power supply.

raw.marginal_note: “Illegal”, “unlawful” and “legally bound to do”
raw.text: The word “illegal” or “unlawful” is applicable to every thing which is an offence, or which is prohibited by law, or which furnishes ground for a civil action: and a person is said to be “legally bound to do” whatever it is illegal or unlawful in him to omit.

436.section_title: Mischief by fire or explosive substance with intent to destroy a house, etc.

raw.marginal_note: Mischief by fire or explosive substance with intent to destroy a house, etc.
raw.text: Whoever commits mischief by fire or any explosive substance, intending to cause, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby cause, the destruction of any building which is ordinarily used as a place for worship, or for the administration of justice, or for the transaction of public affairs, or for education, or art, or for public use or ornament, or as a human dwelling, or as a place for the custody of property, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 10 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

440.section_title: Mischief committed after preparation made for causing death or hurt

raw.marginal_note: Mischief committed after preparation made for causing death or hurt
raw.text: Whoever commits mischief, having made preparation for causing to any person death or hurt or wrongful restraint, or fear of death or of hurt or of wrongful restraint, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 5 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

elements_detail.label: commits_mischief
elements_detail.text: Whoever commits mischief

elements_detail.label: aggravated_preparation
elements_detail.text: Having made preparation for causing to any person death or hurt or wrongful restraint, or fear of death or of hurt or of wrongful restraint

============================================================
Effective: 1872-01-01

Elements of the offence:
  Conjunctive requirement (all_of): every following limb must be proved:
    Physical element (actus reus): Whoever commits mischief
    Circumstance: Having made preparation for causing to any person death or hurt or wrongful restraint, or fear of death or of hurt or of wrongful restraint

Penalty:
  Shall be punished with imprisonment for a term of not less than no time and not more than 5 years.

metadata.summary: Punishes mischief committed after the offender has prepared to cause death, hurt, wrongful restraint, or fear of those consequences to any person.

raw.marginal_note: Criminal trespass
raw.text: Whoever enters into or upon property in the possession of another with intent to commit an offence or to intimidate, insult or annoy any person in possession of such property, or having lawfully entered into or upon such property, unlawfully remains there with intent thereby to intimidate, insult or annoy any such person, or with intent to commit an offence, is said to commit “criminal trespass”.

transpiled.english:
SECTION 441: Criminal trespass
============================================================
Jurisdiction: singapore
  act: Penal Code 1871
  source: https://sso.agc.gov.sg/Act/PC1871?ProvIds=pr441-#pr441-
Effective: 1872-01-01

Definitions:
  "criminal_trespass" means Whoever enters into or upon property in the possession of another with intent to commit an offence or to intimidate, insult or annoy any person in possession of such property, or having lawfully entered into or upon such property, unlawfully remains there with intent thereby to intimidate, insult or annoy any such person, or with intent to commit an offence, is said to commit “criminal trespass”.

Elements of the offence:
  Alternative requirement (any_of): proving one following limb is enough:
    Conjunctive requirement (all_of): every following limb must be proved:
      Physical element (actus reus): Enters into or upon property
      Circumstance: Property is in the possession of another
      Alternative requirement (any_of): proving one following limb is enough:
        Mental element (mens rea): Intent to commit an offence
        Mental element (mens rea): Intent to intimidate, insult or annoy any person in possession of such property
    Conjunctive requirement (all_of): every following limb must be proved:
      Circumstance: Having lawfully entered into or upon such property
      Physical element (actus reus): Unlawfully remains there
      Alternative requirement (any_of): proving one following limb is enough:
        Mental element (mens rea): Intent thereby to intimidate, insult or annoy any such person
        Mental element (mens rea): Intent to commit an offence

metadata.summary: Defines criminal trespass as entry onto property in another's possession, or unlawful remaining after lawful entry, with intent to commit an offence or to intimidate, insult or annoy.

raw.marginal_note: House-breaking
raw.text: Whoever commits criminal trespass by entering into, or remaining in, any building, tent, container or vessel used as a human dwelling, or any building used as a place for worship or as a place for the custody of property, is said to commit “house‑breaking”.

sub_items.text: Explanation. —The introduction of any part of the criminal trespasser’s body is entering sufficient to constitute house-breaking.

raw.marginal_note: House-breaking in order to commit an offence punishable with death
raw.text: Whoever commits house-breaking in order to commit any offence punishable with death, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment for a term not exceeding 15 years, and shall, if he is not sentenced to imprisonment for life, also be liable to fine.

453.section_title: Possession of house-breaking implements or offensive weapons

raw.marginal_note: Possession of house-breaking implements or offensive weapons
raw.text: —(1) Any person who is found — ( a ) armed with any instrument for shooting, stabbing or cutting, or any instrument which, used as a weapon of offence, is likely to cause death, without lawful authority or a lawful purpose; ( b ) having his face covered or otherwise found disguised with intent to commit any offence; or ( c ) equipped with any article or instrument for use in the course of or in connection with any house‑breaking, shall be guilty of an offence and shall on conviction be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 2 years, or with fine, or with both; and any instrument or article, mentioned in paragraph ( a ) or ( c ), found in the possession of that person shall be forfeited. (2) In any prosecution for an offence under subsection (1)( a ), it is sufficient for the prosecution to allege and prove that the accused was found to be armed with any instrument mentioned in paragraph ( a ) and the burden is then on the accused to show that he had lawful authority or a lawful purpose to be so armed. (3) An article or instrument is presumed to be carried with lawful authority if it is carried by —

sub_items.text: —(1) Any person who is found — ( a ) armed with any instrument for shooting, stabbing or cutting, or any instrument which, used as a weapon of offence, is likely to cause death, without lawful authority or a lawful purpose; ( b ) having his face covered or otherwise found disguised with intent to commit any offence; or ( c ) equipped with any article or instrument for use in the course of or in connection with any house‑breaking, shall be guilty of an offence and shall on conviction be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 2 years, or with fine, or with both; and any instrument or article, mentioned in paragraph ( a ) or ( c ), found in the possession of that person shall be forfeited.

sub_items.label: (a)
sub_items.text: armed with any instrument for shooting, stabbing or cutting, or any instrument which, used as a weapon of offence, is likely to cause death, without lawful authority or a lawful purpose;

sub_items.label: (b)
sub_items.text: having his face covered or otherwise found disguised with intent to commit any offence; or

sub_items.label: (c)
sub_items.text: equipped with any article or instrument for use in the course of or in connection with any house‑breaking,

(2) In any prosecution for an offence under subsection (1)( a ), it is sufficient for the prosecution to allege and prove that the accused was found to be armed with any instrument mentioned in paragraph ( a ) and the burden is then on the accused to show that he had lawful authority or a lawful purpose to be so armed.

(3) An article or instrument is presumed to be carried with lawful authority if it is carried by — ( a ) any member of the Singapore Armed Forces, the Singapore Police Force, the Singapore Civil Defence Force or any auxiliary police force created under any written law; ( b ) a forensic specialist or law enforcement officer acting in the course of his duty as such in accordance with the Police Force Act 2004; ( c ) any visiting force lawfully present in Singapore under the provisions of any law relating to visiting forces; or ( d ) any person as part of his official or ceremonial dress on any official or ceremonial occasion.

sub_items.label: (3)(a)
sub_items.text: any member of the Singapore Armed Forces, the Singapore Police Force, the Singapore Civil Defence Force or any auxiliary police force created under any written law;

sub_items.label: (3)(b)
sub_items.text: a forensic specialist or law enforcement officer acting in the course of his duty as such in accordance with the Police Force Act 2004;

sub_items.label: (3)(c)
sub_items.text: any visiting force lawfully present in Singapore under the provisions of any law relating to visiting forces; or

sub_items.label: (3)(d)
sub_items.text: any person as part of his official or ceremonial dress on any official or ceremonial occasion.

462.section_title: Punishment for same offence when committed by person entrusted with custody

raw.marginal_note: Punishment for same offence when committed by person entrusted with custody
raw.text: Whoever, being entrusted with any closed receptacle which contains or which he believes to contain property, without having authority to open the same, dishonestly, or with intent to commit mischief, breaks open or unfastens that receptacle, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to 3 years, or with fine, or with both.

SECTION 463: Forgery
============================================================
Effective: 1872-01-01

Definitions:
  "forgery" means Making a false document with fraudulent intent
  "false_document" means A document that dishonestly or fraudulently purports to be something it is not
  "making" means Creating, altering, or signing a document falsely
  "valuable_security" means Document purporting to create, transfer, or release any right

Elements of the offence:
  Physical element (actus reus): Making a false document or part of document
  Mental element (mens rea): Intent to cause damage or injury to public or person
  Mental element (mens rea): Intent to support any claim or title
  Mental element (mens rea): Intent to cause person to part with property
  Mental element (mens rea): Intent to cause person to enter contract
  Mental element (mens rea): Intent to commit fraud

Penalty:
  Shall be punished with imprisonment for a term of not less than no time and not more than 4 years, or with a fine of not less than S$0.00 and not more than S$10,000.00, or with any combination thereof.

Illustrations:

Illustration example1: A creates a false certificate purporting to be from a university. A has committed forgery.
  Provenance: statutory illustration attached to Section 463.
Illustration example2: A alters the date on a document to make it appear earlier than it was. A has committed forgery.
  Provenance: statutory illustration attached to Section 463.
Illustration example3: A signs B's name on a cheque without B's authority. A has committed forgery.
  Provenance: statutory illustration attached to Section 463.
